AEO.relative_comps: AEO trades at p_fcf ~6.3x and graham_mos 0.76 — among the cheapest in specialty apparel retail, cheaper than ANF (9.7x p_fcf) and well below URBN and PVH (~15-20x). Revenue growth is minimal (+3% YoY) matching peer stagnation across the sector, and margins are thin (net 3.5%) versus ANF's 9.6%, so the discount is partly deserved. Momentum is weak: price is 37% of its 52-wk range and trading just above SMA200 (+19.5 vs current ~$19), suggesting continued pressure. The spread of MoS signals (graham/iv15 green, DCF green at 0.50, EPV negative) points to a value that exists only on normalized earnings — not a clear mispricing given EPS decline of -35%.
AEO.rationale: Core specialty apparel peers by business model (own-brand multi-format retail, similar age/teen demographics, comparable margin structure and inventory cycle) are ANF, URBN, GAP, EXPR. CRI/GIII/PVH are apparel manufacturers that also retail. AEO screens cheapest on p_fcf in this group, but earnings quality is lower than ANF.

ANF.relative_comps: ANF at p_fcf ~9.7x and broad MoS support (DCF 0.61, graham 0.35, OE 0.58, iv15 0.50) looks meaningfully cheap versus the premium-casual peer set; RL trades at 30x p_fcf with comparable gross margins (70% vs ANF's 61%) but ANF actually earns more net margin (9.6% vs RL's 11.6%). Revenue growth (+6.4% YoY, +12.5% 3Y CAGR) is the best in the in-universe apparel cohort (beats AEO/CRI/GIII). Momentum has weakened — price is at 15% of 52-wk range, below SMA200 — but this looks like a temporary drawdown in a fundamentally improving story. The gap versus RL appears a genuine mispricing given ANF's stronger growth trajectory.
ANF.rationale: ANF's Hollister/Abercrombie brands compete directly with URBN's Free People/Anthropologie and Gap's Banana Republic for 20-35 age demographic. Premium lifestyle peers include RL and PVH. ANF and AEO are the closest direct comparables on store footprint and demographic overlap.

BBWI.relative_comps: BBWI at p_fcf ~4.3x is the cheapest specialty personal-care retailer in the peer set — ULTA trades at 15-20x FCF, ELF at 30x+, even SBH (in-universe) at 7.3x. The discount partly reflects revenue contraction (-0.2% YoY), declining EPS (-15%), and a low-growth but highly cash-generative model with best-in-class ROIC of 25.5%. Graham MoS (0.46) and OE MoS (0.63) both signal value; SMA200 at $22 vs current ~$22 is flat, and price sits at 16% of 52-wk range, making it a deep-in-the-range contrarian position. The cash yield and brand monopoly in fragrance/body-care specialty retail justify partial re-rating; the discount is somewhat overdone.
BBWI.rationale: BBWI is a pure-play specialty fragrance/body-care retailer comparable to ULTA (beauty), SBH (Sally Beauty, hair-care specialty), and cosmetics brands EL/COTY/ELF that sell through similar channels. No direct public-market twin exists; LUSH is private. BBWI's captive brand model (near-100% own-label) makes it more comparable to a franchise-like consumer brand than a multi-brand retailer.

BKNG.relative_comps: BKNG at p_fcf ~14.9x is modestly valued relative to its near-monopoly OTA position — EXPE trades at comparable 12-15x but with inferior margins (BKNG net margin 20% vs EXPE ~5-8%) and weaker ROIC (BKNG 32.6% vs EXPE's single digits). AIRBNB has no FCF-based comparable multiple but trades at >40x EBITDA. Revenue growth (+13.4% YoY) and EPS stability confirm the business earns peer-leading returns. Price is at 19% of 52-wk range after a pullback, trading very close to SMA200, and the graham_mos at 0.51 and iv15 at 0.55 suggest fair-to-cheap on normalized pricing power. The valuation gap vs AIRBNB is unjustified given BKNG's superior cash conversion.
BKNG.rationale: BKNG competes directly with EXPE and AIRBNB for lodging/travel booking; secondary peers include TRIP (reviews/booking), regional OTAs like MMYT (India), and DESP (LatAm). The true peer set for capital-intensity and margin structure is BKNG/EXPE as asset-light OTAs — AIRBNB occupies a distinct segment (STR supply) but competes for the same booking wallet.

BMW.DE.relative_comps: BMW at p_fcf 0 (negative FCF period) trades on owner_earnings_mos 0.54 and graham_mos 0.42 — in line with MBG.DE (p_fcf 3.5x, OE_mos 0.79) which screens cheaper, and broadly in line with VOW3.DE (OE_mos 0.80). Revenue is declining (-6.3% YoY) as EV transition costs bite; net margin 5.5% is weaker than historical norms. All three German luxury OEMs trade at structural discounts to TM/HMC (10-14x) because of higher capex intensity, EV write-off risk, and China exposure. BMW's premium brand and financial services arm support a slight re-rating vs MBG but the peer discount is largely justified by cycle risk and capex. Price at just 1% of 52-wk range signals market pessimism.
BMW.DE.rationale: BMW's genuine peer set is the German premium OEM triad (MBG.DE Mercedes-Benz, VOW3.DE Audi/Porsche brands via VW group, PAH3.DE Porsche AG) plus global volume peers GM/F/STLA and Japanese premium makers TM/HMC. BMW occupies the volume-premium sweet spot between mass-market and ultra-luxury.

BYD.relative_comps: BYD (Boyd Gaming) at p_fcf ~18.5x is in-line to modestly rich versus pure-play regional casino peers — MGM trades at 20x+, CZR at 15-18x with higher leverage, RRR at ~15x. BYD's EPS surge (+264% YoY) driven by asset sales/one-offs flatters the current multiple; sustainable ROIC of 12.2% and net margin 45% (elevated by non-recurring items) are strong but may normalize. The SMA200 at $83.7 vs current ~$84 is flat and proximity 88% shows stock near 52-wk high — momentum is constructive but valuation is full. Graham MoS of 0.82 and OE MoS of 0.62 suggest structural undervaluation that the market has largely recognized via the price re-rating.
BYD.rationale: Boyd Gaming is a regional US casino operator competing with MGM Resorts, Caesars Entertainment (CZR), Penn Entertainment (PENN), Red Rock Resorts (RRR), and Churchill Downs (CHDN). LVS/WYNN are Macau/Strip exposed but compete for the same domestic gaming wallet share. BYD's regional focus (Las Vegas locals + regional) makes RRR its closest operational comparable.

CARG.relative_comps: CarGurus at p_fcf ~9.2x is cheap versus its auto-marketplace peers — CARS (Cars.com) trades at 8-12x with inferior margin profile, while CVNA (loss-making) and traditional auto dealers KMX/AN are more capital-intensive. CARG's 89% gross margin reflects its marketplace model (minimal inventory risk); net margin 16.6% is strong. However, revenue 3Y CAGR is deeply negative (-17%) reflecting industry volume headwinds and competition from OEM direct-sales. Price at 8% of 52-wk range and DCF MoS of 0.17 suggest limited margin of safety unless growth re-accelerates. The EPS surge (6.75x YoY) looks transient and tied to mix-shift to higher-margin products. Cheap vs pure-play peers but execution risk is high.
CARG.rationale: CarGurus is an automotive marketplace (lead-gen/marketplace, not inventory holder) competing with Cars.com (CARS), TrueCar (TRUE), and Autobytel. Secondary competitors are dealer-management platforms CDK Global and Reynolds & Reynolds (private). The model is fundamentally different from auto retailers KMX/AN (inventory + financing) but CARG derives value from those dealers as customers.

CCS.relative_comps: Century Communities (CCS) at p_fcf ~13.6x and EPV MoS 0.61 is among the cheaper mid-tier homebuilders, below DHI (13.7x) and PHM (13.5x) but above KBH (12.4x) on an FCF basis. Gross margin 21.4% is thin vs TOL's 26% and PHM's 26.4%, reflecting CCS's entry-level/affordable focus. Revenue is declining (-6.4% YoY) in line with sector trends; EPS fell -53%. The EPV MoS signal is the primary value driver. Momentum is weak (price at 29% of 52-wk range, below SMA200), consistent with sector-wide pressure from elevated rates. CCS screens slightly cheap within the peer group on an earnings basis but the quality/margin gap versus PHM/TOL is real.
CCS.rationale: CCS competes with the full tier of US public homebuilders: DHI (largest by volume), LEN, PHM (operationally most comparable on entry-level mix), KBH, MHO, MTH, GRBK, and luxury-focused TOL. CCS's focus on entry-level/workforce housing in Sun Belt markets aligns it most closely with PHM and KBH.

CHWY.relative_comps: Chewy at p_fcf ~15.6x and iv15_mos 0.49 is modestly valued for a subscription-driven pet-care e-commerce platform, but the multiple is well above Petco (WOOF, trading near distressed levels) and BARK (loss-making). Revenue growth (+6.2% YoY) is decelerating while net margin remains thin at 1.8% — the business still operates on sub-scale economics vs Amazon's bundled Prime offering. ROIC of 3.9% is well below cost of capital. The graham_mos (0.18) provides only weak support. Price at 5% of 52-wk range is deeply depressed. Strategically, CHWY's AutoShip subscription loyalty and vet-tele integration are differentiators, but the path to structural FCF generation justifies a discount vs asset-heavy pet retailers.
CHWY.rationale: Chewy's true peer set is online pet retail: Petco (WOOF), PetSmart (private, PE-owned), BARK (subscription toys/food), PETS (1-800-PetMeds, pharmacy focus), and Amazon as the dominant marketplace competitor. The subscription/autoship model makes CHWY more comparable to subscription consumer platforms than pure-play e-commerce.

CRI.relative_comps: Carter's (CRI) at p_fcf ~19.7x and strong DCF MoS (0.72) looks cheap on a cash flow basis versus branded apparel peers — PVH trades at 8-12x but with different revenue composition, while GIII trades at 5.7x. CRI's brand positioning as the dominant US children's apparel brand (Carter's + OshKosh) warrants a premium but the current p_fcf is elevated by EPS declines (-49%). Net margin 3.2% is depressed vs historical norms of 8-10%; gross margin 45% is structurally high. Revenue growth of +1.9% YoY is stable but unimpressive. Price at 70% of 52-wk range shows recent recovery. The DCF MoS signal suggests this is under-earning relative to intrinsic value — the cheap verdict is contingent on margin normalization.
CRI.rationale: Carter's is the dominant US specialty children's clothing brand; primary peer is OshKosh B'gosh (which CRI itself owns). External comparables are the children's/family apparel segments of PVH (Tommy Hilfiger Kids), GIII (licensed children's lines), and private operators Gerber/Mothercare. CRI's multi-channel model (own stores + wholesale + e-commerce) most closely resembles a scaled specialty retailer.

DECK.relative_comps: Deckers (DECK) at p_fcf ~14.4x and convergence methodology (both Graham 0.60 and OE 0.20 in positive territory) is modestly cheap versus the premium footwear peer set — On Running (ONON) trades at 40-60x, Birkenstock (BIRK) at 30-40x, and CROX at 10-12x. DECK's gross margin 56% and ROIC 28% are best-in-class within the peer set; net margin 18.8% beats NKE (10%) and VFC (sub-5%). Revenue growth +9.4% YoY and 3Y CAGR 14.6% are strong and sustainable (HOKA brand driver). Price at 61% of 52-wk range shows recovery from lows. The discount to ONON/BIRK is substantial and appears a genuine mispricing given DECK's superior cash conversion and brand diversity.
DECK.rationale: Deckers owns HOKA (performance running, direct ONON competitor) and UGG (lifestyle/luxury casual, BIRK/Ugg segment). True peers are performance footwear brands ONON, NKE, SKX, and lifestyle shoe brands BIRK, CROX. DECK's multi-brand structure with premium positioning makes it more comparable to VFC's multi-brand model than to single-brand specialists.

DFH.relative_comps: Dream Finders Homes (DFH) at p_fcf 0 (negative period) uses an asset-light lot-option model that is fundamentally different from capital-intensive peers; EPV MoS 0.66 and OE MoS 0.71 are among the strongest in the homebuilder peer group. Revenue declining slightly (-2.9% YoY) but 3Y CAGR of +9% shows underlying growth. Net margin 5% is thin but consistent with asset-light model; ROIC 11.9% is solid. Price at 12% of 52-wk range is depressed, at significant discount to SMA200 ($19.5 vs current ~$19). The EPV/OE signal cluster suggests DFH is genuinely cheap on normalized earnings, but the micro-cap status and limited float mean the cheapness may persist without a catalyst.
DFH.rationale: DFH is a small-cap asset-light homebuilder competing with GRBK (closest peer by model and size), SmithDouglas Homes (SDHC), and the large-cap builders DHI/LEN/PHM as the market-setters. The asset-light lot-option model reduces land risk; peers by this specific model include GRBK and Taylor Morrison (TMHC on some product lines).

DHI.relative_comps: D.R. Horton at p_fcf ~13.7x is in-line with the large-cap homebuilder peer average — PHM at 13.5x, LEN at an outlier 827x (FCF anomaly), NVR at 20x but highest-quality returns (ROIC >40%). DHI's gross margin 23.7% and net margin 10.5% are middle-of-road; ROIC 15.9% is solid but below NVR. Revenue declined -6.9% YoY matching sector-wide pressure. DCF MoS 0.42 and OE MoS 0.37 show moderate support. Price at 40% of 52-wk range implies market is pricing in prolonged rate pressure. DHI's scale advantage (largest US builder by closings) and entry-level focus suggest the current in-line valuation is fair — NVR commands justified premium for its asset-light model.
DHI.rationale: DHI's true peer set is the US large-cap homebuilder universe: LEN (second-largest), PHM (PulteGroup, operationally most similar), NVR (Richmond American, asset-light premium), TOL (luxury), KBH, MHO. DHI's unique position as the highest-volume entry-level builder with national scale means NVR and PHM are the most operationally comparable.

FR.PA.relative_comps: Valeo (FR.PA) at p_fcf ~6.5x is cheap on a cash-flow basis versus global auto-parts peers — MGA trades at 8x, LEA at 7-8x, BorgWarner (BWA) at 9-11x. However, Valeo's net margin is razor-thin (0.96%) and OE MoS is deeply negative (-0.60), signaling that owner earnings are insufficient to support the valuation — a red flag versus the p_fcf headline. Revenue declined -2.7% YoY and 3Y CAGR is barely positive (+1.4%). The EV-transition risk on interior electronics/sensors is specific to Valeo's product mix (ADAS, thermal management) — potentially a structural headwind. Price at 78% of 52-wk range suggests recent recovery but the OE signal warns against reading the p_fcf cheapness as genuine value.
FR.PA.rationale: Valeo is a French Tier-1 automotive supplier competing with Aptiv (APTV), Lear (LEA), BorgWarner (BWA), Magna (MGA), Visteon (VC), and German peers like Schaeffler (SHA0.DE) and Continental. The closest business-model match on electrification/ADAS electronics is Aptiv and Visteon; on powertrain and thermal systems, BorgWarner and Mahle (private).

GEF.relative_comps: Greif (GEF) at p_fcf 0 (FCF obscured by working capital) trades on strong Graham MoS (0.85) and OE MoS (0.68) — among the best in the industrial packaging peer set. SLGN (Silgan) trades at 9-12x p_fcf; BERY (Berry Global) at 8-10x; GPK (in-universe) is FCF-negative with weaker MoS. GEF's net margin 19.6% is exceptional for a packaging company (AMCR typically runs 5-8%), reflecting a favorable mix of industrial drums and IBCs. Revenue is flat (-1% YoY) but EPS surged (+233%) on cost and mix improvements. Price at 37% of 52-wk range is depressed relative to these quality metrics. The gap between GEF's operational quality and its peer multiples appears a genuine mispricing.
GEF.rationale: Greif manufactures industrial packaging (steel/plastic drums, IBCs, corrugated). True peers by product are Mauser (private, largest rival in industrial drums), BWAY (private), and public comps SLGN, BERY, SEE (Sealed Air, protective packaging), AMCR (Amcor, diversified packaging), and GPK (Graphic Packaging, paperboard). GEF's industrial drum/IBC focus is a niche above commodity corrugated.

GIII.relative_comps: G-III Apparel at p_fcf ~5.7x and strong DCF MoS (0.73) is among the cheapest in branded apparel manufacturing — well below RL (30.6x), OXM (12-15x), and PVH (8-12x). However, revenue declined -7% YoY and EPS crashed -64%, indicating the cheap FCF multiple is flattered by peak earnings in prior periods. Net margin 2.3% is unsustainably thin; the business model (licensed brand manufacturing/wholesale) is inherently lower-quality than owned-brand peers like RL. ROIC 8.8% is mediocre. Price at 86% of 52-wk range suggests the market has already partially re-rated — momentum is constructive. The DCF signal could reflect genuine cheapness if earnings normalize, but the license-dependency risk is real.
GIII.rationale: G-III Apparel manufactures/sells licensed and owned fashion brands (DKNY, Karl Lagerfeld, BCBG, Donna Karan, and licensed Calvin Klein, Tommy Hilfiger, etc.). True peers are other licensing-heavy apparel manufacturers PVH (Calvin Klein/Tommy licensor), Kontoor (Wrangler/Lee, owned brands), Oxford Industries (Tommy Bahama), and HanesBrands (HBI, basics/basics-adjacent). The licensing model creates comparable risk profiles to G-III.

GPK.relative_comps: Graphic Packaging (GPK) at p_fcf 0 (FCF-negative) and Graham MoS only 0.20 is the weakest value signal in the industrial packaging in-universe pair (vs GEF at Graham 0.85). Revenue declined -2.2% YoY and EPS fell -31%; the OE MoS of 0.06 barely registers positive, signaling poor owner earnings quality. Net margin 5.2% is below both GEF (19.6%) and packaging leader AMCR (~7%). ROIC 8.1% is adequate but not exceptional. PKG (Packaging Corp) and IP (International Paper) trade at 12-18x FCF with similar or stronger fundamentals. Price at 12% of 52-wk range is deeply depressed, consistent with the weak fundamental signals. GPK is in-line vs its corrugated/paperboard peers but screens worse than the broader packaging group.
GPK.rationale: Graphic Packaging converts paperboard into consumer packaging (food/beverage cartons). Direct peers by product are WRK (WestRock, corrugated/consumer packaging), IP (International Paper, commercial printing paper and packaging), and PKG (Packaging Corp, corrugated containers). Silgan (SLGN) and Berry Global (BERY) are adjacent in rigid consumer packaging. GEF is an in-universe packaging peer but in industrial drums, not paperboard.

GRBK.relative_comps: Green Brick Partners at p_fcf ~14.5x and convergence methodology (DCF 0.27, EPV 0.39, graham 0.35, OE 0.45) shows moderate value support — comparable to CCS (13.6x) and TMHC (8.9x). GRBK's gross margin 31.2% is superior to most peers (CCS 21.4%, TMHC 23%), and net margin 15.4% with ROIC 16.7% are best-in-class among mid-tier builders. Revenue declined -2.8% YoY (sector-wide), 3Y CAGR modest at 5.1%. Price at 46% of 52-wk range shows partial recovery; Altman-Z of 6.5 indicates strong financial health vs TMHC (3.8) and KBH (3.4). The quality premium (margins, balance sheet) is not fully reflected in the current multiple — GRBK appears cheap relative to quality.
GRBK.rationale: Green Brick Partners is a Texas-focused premium homebuilder most comparable to DFH (asset-light model, similar size), TMHC (Taylor Morrison, entry/move-up Texas), MHO (M/I Homes, similar market positioning), and LGI Homes (LGIH, entry-level Texas/Sun Belt). GRBK's builder-developer hybrid model means its land position is the closest operational match to Century Communities (CCS) among smaller builders.

HOG.relative_comps: Harley-Davidson at p_fcf ~7.2x and strong DCF MoS (0.74) and OE MoS (0.63) is cheap versus the global premium motorcycle peer set — Piaggio (PIRC.MI, in-universe) trades at 6.4x p_fcf but with weaker brand premiums; Polaris (PII) at 8-12x includes recreational vehicles beyond motorcycles. Revenue declined -13.8% YoY and 3Y CAGR is -8%, reflecting aging demographics, channel destocking, and EV transition uncertainty. Net margin 7.6% is below the historical 10-12% range; ROIC 5.3% is low for a brand-driven business. Price at 52% of 52-wk range shows the market is neutral. The DCF MoS signal suggests the market is pricing in terminal decline, which may be too pessimistic given HOG's global brand and LiveWire EV optionality.
HOG.rationale: Harley-Davidson's true peers are premium motorcycle manufacturers: Piaggio/Moto Guzzi (PIRC.MI), KTM AG (listed on Vienna exchange/private), Triumph (private UK), Indian Motorcycle (part of Polaris, PII), and Royal Enfield (Eicher Motors, India). Honda/Yamaha/Kawasaki make bikes across all segments but compete in HOG's touring/cruiser core. The closest market-positioning peer is Indian Motorcycle (also cruiser/touring US-focused).

HRB.relative_comps: H&R Block at p_fcf ~8.7x and multi-method MoS support (DCF 0.63, OE 0.63, graham 0.71) is one of the most attractively valued consumer services franchises in the universe. The closest public peer for tax software is INTU (TurboTax), which trades at 40-50x FCF with superior growth but very different capital intensity — HRB's ROIC of 25.1% and net margin 16.1% are exceptional for a physical-services business. Revenue growth is low (+4.2% YoY) reflecting a mature market, but EPS grew +6.6% and the cash generation is highly predictable. Price at 33% of 52-wk range and SMA200 at $41 vs current ~$41 suggests stable sideways trading. The franchise monopoly on assisted tax filing (duopoly with Jackson Hewitt/Liberty) is not reflected in a 8.7x FCF multiple.
HRB.rationale: H&R Block operates assisted tax preparation (retail locations + DIY software) competing with Jackson Hewitt and Liberty Tax (both private), and digital peers INTU (TurboTax, dominant DIY), FreeTaxUSA, and Credit Karma Tax. The physical franchise model has no true public equivalent at scale; INTU is the closest large-cap comparable but operates primarily in DIY and small-business accounting, not assisted prep.

IVG.MI.relative_comps: IVG.MI (Iveco Group) is a commercial vehicle OEM — trucks, buses, defence vehicles — that competes directly with TRATON (VOW3.DE subsidiary), Daimler Truck (not screened), Volvo Trucks and PACCAR; BMW.DE and MBG.DE are passenger car peers only at the parent-group level, not in the same segment. At p_fcf ~4.1x and positive DCF/Graham MoS (~0.18/0.61), IVG is priced materially cheaper than STLA/MBG equivalents (~6-7x) despite comparable low single-digit net margins (~2%); its revenue decline (-12% YoY) and near-zero price momentum (proximity_52wk 0.6%) signal no near-term catalyst, but the valuation gap to truck OEM peers is real. The discount is partially justified by the commercial-vehicle cycle downturn and thin roic (~5%), but the MoS spread suggests genuine cheapness versus the peer set if free cash flow normalises.
IVG.MI.rationale: Iveco Group is a commercial/defence vehicle OEM (not a passenger-car maker); true peers are Daimler Truck, TRATON/MAN, Volvo Trucks, and PACCAR — plus STLA/RNO at the European integrated-OEM level. BMW.DE and MBG.DE are in-universe proxies but operate in a structurally higher-margin passenger segment.

KBH.relative_comps: KBH (KB Home) is a US entry-level/first-move-up production builder with an asset-light, build-to-order model, competing directly with DHI, LEN, PHM, TOL, MHO, MTH, TMHC and GRBK, all of which appear in-universe. KBH trades at p_fcf ~12.4x with strong multi-method MoS (DCF 0.70, EPV 0.58, owner 0.57), making it one of the cheapest in the homebuilder cohort where peers range from ~8.9x (TMHC) to ~52x (MTH); KBH's gross margin (~19%) is below PHM (~26%), TOL (~26%) and MHO (~23%) — reflecting its entry-level mix — but its roic (~10%) and DCF MoS are competitive. Revenue decline (-10% YoY) is in line with the sector; momentum is mid-range (prox_52wk 32%). The valuation gap to PHM/TOL is partially justified by margin structure but KBH appears cheap on absolute FCF and EPV.
KBH.rationale: KBH is a pure-play US production homebuilder with a low-income/entry-level focus; the entire in-universe residential construction cluster (DHI, LEN, PHM, TOL, MHO, MTH, TMHC, GRBK, CCS, DFH) forms the natural peer set.

KSS.relative_comps: KSS (Kohl's) is a US mid-tier department/off-price department store competing with Macy's (M), Nordstrom (JWN), Dillard's (DDS) and off-price players TJX and ROST at the margin; none of these peers appear in-universe. KSS trades at an extreme discount — p_fcf ~1.6x and strong owner_earnings MoS (0.72) and DCF MoS (0.78) — reflecting deep structural concern about secular decline in department store traffic and a heavily leveraged balance sheet (altman_z 1.68, borderline distress zone). Gross margin (~40.6%) is in line with sector peers, but net margin (~1.8%) and roic (~5%) confirm operational fragility; revenue is contracting (-4.3% YoY, -5% CAGR). The optically cheap multiple is a value trap signal: the FCF multiple is low because the business is burning through real estate optionality and the MoS estimates may not capture structural deterioration. Price momentum is positive (prox_52wk 44%) — likely a mean-reversion bounce, not a fundamental re-rate.
KSS.rationale: KSS is a traditional US department store; real peers are other department/off-price chains (Macy's, Nordstrom, Dillard's, TJX, ROST). The screener universe lacks direct department-store comparables, making out-of-universe peer knowledge essential.

LEG.relative_comps: LEG (Leggett & Platt) makes diversified components — bedding springs, wire, furniture parts, flooring underlayment — competing with Sleep Number (SNBR), HNI, Knoll (KNL), and packaging/materials peers like SON; no direct in-universe comp. At p_fcf ~5.0x and DCF MoS 0.67 / Graham MoS 0.30, LEG looks statistically cheap, but the business is in structural decline: revenue -7.4% YoY, -7.6% 3-yr CAGR, gross margin at a thin 18%, and the company slashed its dividend in 2024 after years of over-leverage. ROIC (~8%) is below cost of capital on a fully-loaded basis given the leverage; altman_z (2.92) is uninspiring. The discount to intrinsic value is real on static metrics but the earnings power is structurally eroding — cheap on a point-in-time basis but likely a value trap without a restructuring catalyst. Momentum is moderate (prox_52wk 42%).
LEG.rationale: LEG is a diversified industrial components manufacturer for bedding, furniture and flooring — true peers are other commodity-component makers (SNBR, HNI, SON) and industrial conglomerates with similar end-markets. The peer set is largely outside this universe.

LEN.relative_comps: LEN (Lennar) is the second-largest US homebuilder by volume, competing across entry-level to luxury with DHI, PHM, TOL, KBH and NVR (not screened). LEN's p_fcf is anomalously high (~828x) — almost certainly a working-capital timing distortion or a period where FCF was near-zero due to land spend — while DCF (0.48) and EPV (0.43) MoS are solid; the more reliable valuation signal is the multi-method MoS consensus, which suggests fair-to-cheap. Gross margin (~10%) is the lowest in the cohort (PHM: 26%, TOL: 26%, MHO: 23%), partly because LEN reports land costs differently and has been aggressively using lot options, compressing reported gross margin. Net margin (6.1%) and roic (11.3%) are mid-cohort. Revenue -3.5% YoY follows the sector; momentum is soft (prox_52wk 15%). LEN appears in-line to modestly cheap on EPV/DCF versus the homebuilder peer group.
LEN.rationale: LEN is a large-cap production homebuilder; peers are the full US public homebuilder group (DHI, PHM, TOL, KBH, NVR, MHO, MTH, TMHC). The anomalous p_fcf distorts the screen — EPV and DCF MoS are more reliable signals.

MBG.DE.relative_comps: MBG.DE (Mercedes-Benz) is a premium passenger-car and van OEM competing with BMW, Stellantis, Renault and at the top end Porsche/Ferrari; BMW.DE and VOW3.DE are in-universe. MBG is the cheapest of the three at p_fcf ~3.5x (BMW: ~0 reported, VOW3: ~0 reported — both distorted) and multi-method MoS supporting strong DCF/owner consensus (0.59/0.79). Gross margin (~17%) and net margin (~3.9%) are slightly above VOW3 (16.9%/2.3%) but below BMW's typical 5-6% net margin; roic (~4.4%) is lowest of the three. Revenue is declining (-9.2% YoY vs -8.4% VOW3, -6.3% BMW) and all three sit near 52-week lows (prox_52wk 4%). The discount is legitimate given cyclical demand weakness, EV transition capex and China exposure, but MBG's free cash generation in a normalised environment should support the deep MoS readings — cheap vs the European premium auto peer group on fundamentals.
MBG.DE.rationale: MBG.DE is a European premium passenger car and van OEM; true peers are BMW.DE, VOW3.DE (both in-universe), Stellantis (STLA), Renault (RNO.PA), and at the halo end Ferrari (RACE.MI). Ford/GM are secondary comps at the global OEM level.

MHO.relative_comps: MHO (M/I Homes) is a mid-size US move-up and luxury homebuilder competing with PHM, TOL, MTH and TMHC in the same geography-and-price tier; all are in-universe. MHO trades at p_fcf ~31x (elevated vs cohort median ~13x) but DCF (0.51) and EPV (0.52) MoS are solid — the FCF distortion likely reflects timing of land investment. Gross margin (~23%) and net margin (~9.1%) sit between KBH (~19%/6.9%) and PHM (~26%/12.8%); roic (~12.3%) is among the better in the group. Revenue -1.9% YoY is the mildest contraction in the cohort. Price momentum is strong (prox_52wk 61%), suggesting the market already recognises quality — MHO trades in-line to modestly rich on FCF multiple vs peers, but DCF/EPV do not confirm overvaluation.
MHO.rationale: MHO is a US regional production homebuilder focused on move-up buyers; the entire screened residential construction cohort (PHM, TOL, KBH, MTH, TMHC, LEN, GRBK, DHI) forms its direct peer group.

MTH.relative_comps: MTH (Meritage Homes) is a US production builder focused on energy-efficient entry-to-move-up homes, competing with PHM, KBH, TOL and TMHC in the Sun Belt. At p_fcf ~52x (again, FCF-timing distortion in a land-heavy cycle) but EPV MoS 0.56 and owner 0.51 are solid; DCF MoS (0.14) is the weakest in the homebuilder cohort here. Gross margin (~19.7%) and net margin (~7.7%) are mid-cohort; roic (~13.3%) is above average — among the best in the peer group. Revenue -8.4% YoY is a sharper contraction than TMHC or TOL. Momentum is moderate (prox_52wk 39%). MTH looks fairly valued to modestly cheap on EPV/owner_earnings versus peers, but the DCF signal is the weakest, and execution risk remains elevated in a high-rate environment.
MTH.rationale: MTH is a US production homebuilder with a Sun Belt/energy-efficient niche; peers are the full domestic homebuilder group (PHM, TOL, KBH, TMHC, LEN, DHI, NVR, MHO), all of which are in-universe or near-universe.

PAH3.DE.relative_comps: PAH3.DE (Porsche Automobil Holding SE) is a listed holding company whose primary asset is a ~53% stake in Volkswagen AG (VOW3.DE); it is not an auto OEM but an investment holding vehicle competing structurally with other European family-holding companies (EXOR.MI, HAL.AS) and conglomerate-discount plays. At p_fcf ~6.2x and strong DCF/Graham MoS (0.69/0.75), PAH3 trades at a significant holding-company discount to the underlying NAV of its VOW3 stake. Revenue=0 (pure holding, no operating revenue reported); net margin ~97% is entirely dividend/income from VW. The holding-company discount (typically 20-40% to NAV in European structures) is structural and unlikely to close without a catalyst — the optically strong MoS reflects the deep discount to VW's already-depressed equity, not a standalone operating business. Price is near its 52-week low (prox_52wk 3%), in line with VW/MBG weakness.
PAH3.DE.rationale: PAH3.DE is a holding company, not an auto OEM; real peers are listed holding companies (EXOR.MI, HAL.AS) and controlling-shareholder vehicles. The auto sector framing overstates its operating-business character — it is a pure-play VW equity proxy trading at a structural holding-company discount.

PHM.relative_comps: PHM (PulteGroup) is a large diversified US homebuilder with strong brand positioning across first-time, move-up and active-adult segments, competing with DHI, LEN, TOL and KBH. PHM is the highest-quality operator in the in-universe cohort: gross margin (~26.4%) and net margin (~12.8%) are the best among the screened homebuilders; roic (~18.7%) is the highest in the group by a wide margin. At p_fcf ~13.5x and DCF/EPV/owner MoS all positive (0.44/0.36/0.50), PHM is priced in-line with its quality premium. Revenue -3.5% YoY is sector-average; momentum (prox_52wk 44%) is mid-range. The valuation premium to KBH and TMHC is justified by structurally superior margins and returns — PHM is in-line to modestly rich versus the peer group on a quality-adjusted basis.
PHM.rationale: PHM is a top-3 US homebuilder by deliveries; the full in-universe residential construction cohort (DHI, LEN, TOL, KBH, MHO, MTH, TMHC, GRBK, CCS, DFH) is its direct peer set, with NVR (not screened) as the highest-quality reference point.

PIRC.MI.relative_comps: PIRC.MI (Pirelli & C.) is a premium tire manufacturer competing with Michelin (ML.PA, not screened), Continental (CON.DE, not screened) and Bridgestone (5108.T, not screened); within the screened universe SHA0.DE (Schaeffler) and VC (Visteon) are auto-parts peers but in different segments. Pirelli operates in the high-value premium/ultra-high-performance tire niche with structurally better margins (~32.8% gross, 7.3% net) than typical auto-parts peers — SHA0.DE has gross margin ~22% and net ~1.9%. At p_fcf ~6.4x and DCF MoS 0.49 / Graham MoS 0.42, PIRC is cheap relative to the premium-tire analog of Michelin (typically 10-15x FCF). Revenue growth is essentially flat (+0.04%) with modest earnings growth (+6.4%); momentum is mid-range (prox_52wk 48%). The discount to global tire peers like Michelin is partly due to the Chinese state-owned Sinochem stake overhang, which creates governance risk. PIRC looks genuinely cheap vs the true tire peer set.
PIRC.MI.rationale: PIRC.MI is a global premium tire manufacturer; true peers are Michelin (ML.PA), Continental (CON.DE), Bridgestone and Nokian Tyres — not generic auto-parts makers. Schaeffler (SHA0.DE) and Visteon (VC) are in-universe but structurally different businesses.

PUIG.MC.relative_comps: PUIG.MC (Puig Brands) is a Spanish luxury/prestige beauty and fashion holding company with iconic fragrance brands (Carolina Herrera, Rabanne, Jean Paul Gaultier, Charlotte Tilbury); its true peers are Coty, Interparfums and the fragrance divisions of L'Oreal (OR.PA) and Richemont (CFR.SW). No direct in-universe peer exists. PUIG trades at p_fcf ~11x and Graham MoS 0.63, suggesting moderate cheapness; gross margin (~70.4%) confirms the luxury-beauty positioning and is in line with the sector. Revenue growth +5.3% YoY and 3yr CAGR +11.7% are solid; net margin (~11.8%) and roic (~7%) are below L'Oreal/Estee Lauder quality levels but reasonable for a recently-IPO'd family-controlled entity. Momentum is neutral (prox_52wk 51%). PUIG trades at a discount to luxury beauty comps (Coty trades at higher multiples despite lower margins), partly due to the IPO discount and family-control premium; the MoS spread is real but limited.
PUIG.MC.rationale: PUIG.MC is a prestige fragrance and luxury fashion house (not a generic consumer goods company); true peers are Interparfums, Coty, L'Oreal's luxury division, and at the premium end LVMH/Richemont fragrance segments. No equivalent in the screened universe.

RL.relative_comps: RL (Ralph Lauren) is an American aspirational luxury/lifestyle brand competing with PVH, VFC, Tapestry and Capri at the accessible luxury tier and with Burberry/Hugo Boss at the international aspirational end; no direct in-universe peer exists (GIII is a manufacturer-licensor, not a brand peer). RL trades at p_fcf ~30.6x and weak DCF MoS (-0.39) / borderline Graham MoS (0.28), but strong ev_gross_profit signal; gross margin (~69.9%) and net margin (~11.6%) are genuinely excellent and roic (~12.8%) is competitive. Revenue growth +14.6% YoY (strong, boosted by international expansion and pricing) and 3yr CAGR +8% are above peer rates. Momentum is excellent (prox_52wk 80%). RL is priced in-line to modestly rich: the premium multiple is largely justified by superior margins, accelerating revenue and brand equity, but the multiple leaves little room for error versus cheaper accessible-luxury peers.
RL.rationale: RL is an American aspirational luxury brand; true peers are PVH, VFC, Tapestry, Capri Holdings and European aspirational peers (Hugo Boss, Burberry). GIII (in-universe) is a licensing/manufacturing partner, not a brand peer.

RVLV.relative_comps: RVLV (Revolve Group) is an online fashion platform targeting millennial/Gen-Z premium consumers, competing with ASOS, Boohoo, Farfetch (in wind-down) and traditional off-price retailers at the margin; ANF (in-universe) is the closest proxy. RVLV trades at p_fcf ~28.4x with negative DCF and EPV MoS (-0.45/-0.80) — the FCF multiple is elevated relative to its modest 5% net margin. Gross margin (~53.5%) is decent but roic (~13.4%) is supported largely by an asset-light model. Revenue growth +8.5% YoY is positive but below the expectation embedded in a 28x FCF multiple. Price momentum is weak (prox_52wk 11%), suggesting the market is skeptical. Relative to ANF (in-universe, p_fcf ~9.7x, better margins) and other online apparel peers, RVLV looks rich on FCF and is not justified by commensurate growth or margin advantage.
RVLV.rationale: RVLV is a pure-play online premium fashion marketplace; true peers are ASOS, Boohoo and other digitally-native fashion players. ANF (in-universe) is the best available proxy — RVLV trades at a significant premium to ANF despite inferior margin quality.

SBH.relative_comps: SBH (Sally Beauty Holdings) is a beauty supply retailer and distributor targeting professional stylists and DIY consumers, competing primarily with Ulta Beauty (ULTA, not screened) and Regis Corporation; no direct in-universe peer. At p_fcf ~7.3x and strong owner_earnings MoS (0.69) and DCF MoS (0.59), SBH is statistically cheap; gross margin (~51.6%) is solid for retail and net margin (~5.3%) is acceptable. Revenue is essentially flat (-0.4% YoY, -1% 3yr CAGR) — a mature, no-growth business. ROIC (~10.9%) is reasonable. Momentum is moderate (prox_52wk 38%). Relative to ULTA (trades at 15-20x FCF with high growth), SBH is a deep discount for a structurally stagnant business — the cheap multiple reflects correctly the zero-growth profile, but on an absolute cash flow basis the discount vs stagnant peers is real and likely excessive.
SBH.rationale: SBH is a specialty beauty supply retailer; the correct peer group is ULTA Beauty, Regis and professional beauty distributors. No direct in-universe comparable exists — the cheapness assessment is driven by absolute FCF yield vs out-of-universe beauty retail peers.

SHA0.DE.relative_comps: SHA0.DE (Schaeffler) is a German precision automotive and industrial bearings/motion technology manufacturer competing with Continental (CON.DE, not screened), BorgWarner (BWA, not screened) and in-universe peers PIRC.MI and VC. At p_fcf ~14.6x and weak DCF MoS (-0.10) / moderate Graham/owner MoS (0.35/0.46), SHA0 is priced in-line to modestly cheap; gross margin (~22%) and net margin (~1.9%) are the weakest in the auto-parts cohort. ROIC (~1.8%) is well below cost of capital — a serious red flag. Revenue grew +3.2% YoY but eps contracted sharply (-45%), reflecting the EV transition restructuring costs. Momentum is strong (prox_52wk 71%), suggesting recent news has re-rated the stock. Schaeffler is correctly priced at a discount to PIRC (better margin, FCF clarity) and is approximately in-line with generic auto-parts peers given the roic problem.
SHA0.DE.rationale: SHA0.DE is an automotive precision components and bearings maker; real peers are Continental, BorgWarner, ZF Friedrichshafen (private), Aptiv and in-universe peers PIRC.MI and VC. The EV transition is a structural headwind common to all traditional drivetrain suppliers.

TMHC.relative_comps: TMHC (Taylor Morrison Home) is a US production homebuilder focused on move-up and active-adult buyers, competing with PHM, TOL, MTH and MHO in similar geographic and product-tier markets. TMHC trades at p_fcf ~8.9x — the second cheapest in the homebuilder cohort after KBH — with solid DCF (0.43) and EPV (0.43) MoS. Gross margin (~23%) is mid-cohort; net margin (~9.6%) and roic (~11.5%) are competitive. Revenue is essentially flat (-0.6% YoY), the best relative performance in the cohort. Price momentum is excellent (prox_52wk 95%), the strongest in the group, suggesting the market is already recognising the relative value. TMHC appears in-line to modestly cheap given its combination of low FCF multiple, decent margins and strong momentum — not as cheap as KBH on raw multiple but better momentum confirms market awareness.
TMHC.rationale: TMHC is a US production homebuilder in the move-up/active-adult segment; all screened residential construction peers (PHM, TOL, KBH, MTH, MHO, LEN, DHI, GRBK) are the direct competitor set.

TOL.relative_comps: TOL (Toll Brothers) is the only large-scale US luxury homebuilder, competing primarily with NVR (not screened, the quality standard) and with PHM/LEN at the upper move-up tier; no pure luxury homebuilder peer exists at scale. TOL trades at p_fcf ~13.4x with multi-method MoS support (DCF 0.43, EPV 0.34, owner 0.41, Graham 0.39) — a broad consensus, suggesting fair pricing not cheapness. Gross margin (~26.0%) and net margin (~12.3%) are in line with PHM, the best comps in the cohort; roic (~11.7%) is good but below PHM (~18.7%). Revenue growth +1.1% YoY is the best in the homebuilder cohort alongside TMHC. Momentum is moderate (prox_52wk 53%). TOL is priced in-line with its quality tier — not cheap enough relative to PHM and not carrying any luxury brand premium that would justify a richer multiple.
TOL.rationale: TOL is the US luxury production homebuilder; the closest true peer is NVR (not screened) at the quality and execution level, and the full domestic homebuilder cohort (PHM, LEN, KBH, MHO, MTH, TMHC, DHI) at the market level.

TUI1.DE.relative_comps: TUI1.DE (TUI AG) is an integrated European leisure travel group — tour operator, airline, hotels, cruise ships — with no direct in-universe peer; BKNG (in-universe) is an OTA, not a vertically integrated operator. TUI's true peers are Thomas Cook (dissolved), EasyJet/Ryanair at the airline leg, and NCLH/RCL at the cruise leg. At p_fcf ~3.6x and strong DCF/owner MoS (0.76/0.76), TUI looks statistically very cheap; however, gross margin (~9.2%) and net margin (~2.6%) are structurally thin for an asset-heavy, capital-intensive integrated model, and roic (~0.4%) is essentially nil — not a quality business. Revenue growth +4.4% YoY and 3yr CAGR +13.5% reflect post-COVID recovery, not underlying organic growth. Momentum is weak (prox_52wk 22%). TUI is cheap on FCF but the quality is low — a classic value trap candidate unless the post-COVID normalisation drives further margin recovery.
TUI1.DE.rationale: TUI1.DE is an integrated European leisure travel conglomerate (tour operator + airline + hotels + cruise); BKNG (in-universe) is an OTA with a fundamentally different business model. True peers are integrated package-holiday operators (Thomas Cook predecessor, DER Touristik, FTI) and the cruise/hotel segments (NCLH, RCL, NH Hotels).

VC.relative_comps: VC (Visteon Corporation) designs and manufactures digital cockpit electronics (instrument clusters, infotainment, ADAS displays) for global OEMs — a structurally different segment from bearings/tires but in-universe peers SHA0.DE and PIRC.MI are the closest available proxies. True peers are Aptiv (APTV, not screened) and Forvia's cockpit division. VC trades at p_fcf ~11.7x with DCF MoS 0.21, Graham MoS 0.68 and owner MoS 0.30 — a moderate consensus. Gross margin (~14.1%) is low but characteristic of Tier-1 automotive electronics; net margin (~5.3%) and roic (~8.7%) are mid-cohort. Revenue decline -2.5% YoY reflects OEM production cuts, not share loss. Momentum is strong (prox_52wk 75%). Versus SHA0.DE (weaker roic, lower margin), VC looks better-quality; versus PIRC (stronger margins, better FCF), VC is cheaper. The overall multiple looks in-line with the Tier-1 auto-electronics peer group.
VC.rationale: VC is a Tier-1 automotive cockpit electronics supplier; real peers are Aptiv (cockpit/software), Forvia, Continental's interior division and HARMAN (Samsung subsidiary). SHA0.DE and PIRC.MI are the only available in-universe proxies, though they operate in different auto-parts segments.

ACI.relative_comps: ACI (Albertsons) trades at p_fcf ~17x with a near-zero net margin (0.26%) and severely deteriorating EPS (-75.6% YoY), placing it at a rich valuation vs. the grocery peer set where Kroger (KR) fetches ~11-13x FCF with meaningfully higher throughput margins and Sprouts (SFM) earns premium multiples on structurally superior organic-format margins (gross ~35%). ACI's owner-earnings MoS of 0.73 is the one method that supports it — but that reflects real-estate-inflated owner earnings rather than operating quality; ROIC of 6.4% trails the cost of capital. The valuation gap vs. Kroger and SFM is NOT justified: ACI lacks SFM's format differentiation and trails KR on scale economies, while the pending merger limbo has created noise in reported earnings that flatters OE-based metrics. Momentum is weak (proximity_52wk = 13%, well below SMA200 of $17.54), consistent with a stock pricing in competitive margin pressure.
ACI.rationale: ACI is a conventional US supermarket chain; true comps are Kroger, Sprouts, Grocery Outlet, Casey's General (convenience grocery), Weis Markets, Winn-Dixie parent (Southeastern Grocers), Village Super Market, and Performance Food Group on the wholesale/distribution end. SFM and GO are the clearest format-comparable benchmarks.

BRBR.relative_comps: BRBR (BellRing Brands — Premier Protein) is the standout value in this group: p_fcf of just 4.4x combined with the highest ROIC in the Consumer Defensive cohort (36.7%), 16% revenue growth, a 19% three-year CAGR, and a graham_revised MoS of 0.83 signal a genuinely cheap, high-quality compounder. Vs. closest branded-nutrition peers — Simply Good Foods (SMPL, p_fcf ~7.7x, ROIC ~6.8%), Post Holdings (POST, p_fcf ~14x), and Nomad Foods (NOMD, p_fcf ~12x) — BRBR is at a clear discount despite far superior ROIC and growth trajectory. The gap is NOT justified by quality: BRBR has a tighter, more focused brand portfolio (Premier Protein, Dymatize) with repeat purchase / pantry-loading dynamics, substantially better capital turn, and a secular tailwind from high-protein consumer preference. The only caveat is proximity_52wk = 1.2%, meaning the stock is at a 52-week low — which creates a potential technical headwind but reinforces the value case.
BRBR.rationale: BRBR is a pure-play active-nutrition / protein-shake brand; true comps are Simply Good Foods (SMPL — Quest/Atkins), Vital Farms (VITL — premium branded), Freshpet (FRPT — premium pet/natural, consumer-engagement model), Nomad Foods (NOMD — Birds Eye, European frozen), Post Holdings (POST — diversified branded packaged), Lancaster Colony (LANC — premium condiments), and Ingredion (INGR — B2B ingredient supplier to branded foods). CALM is in-universe and shares a Consumer Defensive slot for cross-reference.

CALM.relative_comps: CALM (Cal-Maine Foods) is the cheapest name in the cohort at p_fcf ~3.5x and is validated by near-universal method confluence (DCF, EPV, Graham, OE all deeply positive), driven by an extraordinary avian-influenza-cycle windfall: revenue +83% YoY, EPS +338%, net margin 28.6%, ROIC 20.9% — metrics that sit many multiples above its structurally-cyclical egg-producer peers. Against Sanderson Farms (acquired by Cargill/Continental Grain), Tyson Foods (TSN, p_fcf ~12-16x, net margin 0-2%), and Hormel (HRL, p_fcf ~18x, net margin 6-8%), CALM screens as wildly cheap — but the valuation discount is PARTIALLY justified because CALM's current margins are at a historical peak driven by the HPAI egg shortage, and mean reversion is certain as the flock recovers. The proximity_52wk of only 6.8% suggests the market has begun pricing in normalization; however, CALM's balance sheet is essentially debt-free and cash-generative enough that the trough valuation is unlikely to be painful. A cycle-adjusted p_fcf of ~8-10x still appears cheap versus TSN.
CALM.rationale: CALM is the largest US egg producer; true comps are Pilgrim's Pride (PPC — in-universe), Sanderson Farms (now private under Cargill/Continental Grain JV), Tyson Foods (TSN — diversified protein), Hormel Foods (HRL — processed meats/protein), Seaboard Corp (SEB — pork/poultry), and on the premium side Vital Farms (VITL — pasture-raised eggs/butter). INGR is in-universe for reference as a grain/starch processing comparison on margins.

INGR.relative_comps: INGR (Ingredion) is an ingredient-science / specialty-starch business that screens at p_fcf ~12.8x with strong method convergence (DCF 0.49, Graham 0.71, iv15 0.59); revenue is modestly declining (-2.8% YoY, -3.1% 3Y CAGR) but EPS is recovering (+15% YoY) as raw material costs ease. Versus Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM, p_fcf 9-12x, gross margin ~10%), Bunge (BUNG, p_fcf ~7x, gross margin ~8%), and IFF (International Flavors & Fragrances, p_fcf ~16x, gross margin ~35%), INGR occupies the middle ground: meaningfully better margin quality than commodity grain processors (gross margin 25.3% vs. ADM/BUNG at 8-10%) and cheaper than specialty-ingredient or flavor peers like IFF or Balchem. ROIC of 9.0% is respectable but not exceptional; the discount vs. IFF/Balchem is justified by Ingredion's higher commodity-input sensitivity and lower pricing power in core starches. Overall, INGR screens in-line to mildly cheap vs. its true mid-tier ingredient-science peer set.
INGR.rationale: Ingredion is a specialty ingredient and starch supplier to food, beverage, paper and industrial customers; true comps are Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM — global ag processing), Bunge (BUNG — oilseed/grain processing), Agrana (AGX.VI — starch/fruit/sugar, Vienna-listed), Balchem (BALCHEM — microencapsulation specialty), RPM International (RPM — specialty chemicals adjacent), IFF (International Flavors & Fragrances — flavor/texture ingredients), and Simply Good Foods (SMPL — branded end-market that buys from suppliers like INGR).

LW.relative_comps: LW (Lamb Weston — frozen potato/french fry manufacturer) trades at p_fcf ~26x with a graham_revised MoS of 0.85 but near-zero revenue growth (-0.3% YoY) and a severely impaired EPS (-49.8% YoY) as cost inflation and restaurant-traffic softness squeezed the business; net margin at 5.5% is well below its historical 8-10% range. Versus McCain Foods (private, key comp), Simplot (private), and publicly-traded restaurant supply adjacent names, LW is structurally the global #2 in frozen potato with ~50% of its sales to QSR chains — a high-quality oligopoly position. At p_fcf 26x vs. PPC at 10.8x, INGR at 12.8x, or Post at ~14x, LW appears rich on current earnings — but the EPS trough is visible and the MoS based on normalized Graham metrics is wide (0.85). The richness is thus partly illusory: investors are pricing recovery to a 9-10% net margin, which is reasonable given the oligopoly structure. Momentum is poor (proximity_52wk 15.3%, below SMA200 of $50.52), suggesting the market has not yet priced in the recovery with conviction.
LW.rationale: Lamb Weston is a global frozen potato / french fry manufacturer selling to QSR and foodservice channels; true comps are McCain Foods (private — global #1 in frozen potato), J.R. Simplot (private — integrated potato/fertilizer), Nomad Foods (NOMD — Birds Eye frozen vegetables, European), Harvest Natural Resources (HNR, tangential), Post Holdings (POST — diversified food manufacturing), Pilgrim's Pride (PPC — QSR protein supply chain), McCormick (MKC — condiment/sauce partner in QSR supply chain), and Hormel (HRL — processed/convenience food adjacent). SMPL and INGR are in-universe for cross-sector comparison.

PPC.relative_comps: PPC (Pilgrim's Pride — chicken processing) trades at p_fcf ~10.8x with a graham_revised MoS of 0.75 and mid-single-digit revenue growth (+3.5% YoY); gross margin is thin at 12.7% and net margin at 5.9%, both typical of commodity protein processing but below CALM's current extraordinary margins. Versus Tyson Foods (TSN, p_fcf ~12-16x, structurally larger and more diversified) and Hormel (HRL, p_fcf ~18x, higher-value processed meats), PPC screens as cheap-to-in-line: lower multiple, acceptable ROIC (9.9%), and better growth visibility than HRL given QSR volume commitments. PPC's majority owner JBS (Brazilian protein giant) provides balance-sheet support and distribution synergies that partially justify a discount to TSN's independent conglomerate premium. The gap vs. CALM (p_fcf 3.5x) reflects CALM's extraordinary avian-flu windfall rather than structural superiority. Overall, PPC is modestly cheap vs. the diversified protein peer set at current pricing.
PPC.rationale: PPC (Pilgrim's Pride) is a vertically integrated chicken processor and the largest US poultry company by volume (majority-owned by JBS); true comps are Cal-Maine Foods (CALM — in-universe, egg producer on same supply chain), Tyson Foods (TSN — diversified beef/pork/chicken), Hormel Foods (HRL — value-added processed protein), Sanderson Farms (now private, JV with Cargill/Continental Grain), Seaboard Corp (SEB — pork/ocean shipping), WH Group (288.HK — Smithfield parent, global pork), and Nomad Foods (NOMD — European frozen protein). BRBR is in-universe for cross-consumer-defensive comparison.

RI.PA.relative_comps: RI.PA (Pernod Ricard) is a global spirits major — owner of Absolut, Jameson, Chivas, Martell — that trades at p_fcf ~12.9x with a moderate EPV MoS (-0.07, near-zero) and owner-earnings MoS of 0.30; revenue fell -5.5% YoY reflecting China destocking and US consumer softness, and gross margin at 59.5% is in the ballpark for premium spirits (peers range 55-70%). Against Diageo (DEO, p_fcf ~18-22x, gross margin 60%, premium brand portfolio), Brown-Forman (BF.B, p/fcf ~22-26x, gross margin 72%), and Remy Cointreau (RCO.PA, p_fcf ~20-25x, hyper-luxury cognac), Pernod screens as cheap: lower multiple, similar or better geographic diversification, but temporarily impaired by China/US destocking cycles. The discount vs. Diageo (~40% on FCF) is partly justified by Diageo's superior portfolio mix (scotch/beer premiumization) and stronger US on-premise positioning, but the magnitude looks excessive given Pernod's cognac (Martell) recovery optionality. Momentum is poor (proximity_52wk 7.2%, well below SMA200 of €76.48), confirming market skepticism about the destocking timeline.
RI.PA.rationale: Pernod Ricard is a global premium spirits conglomerate (Absolut, Jameson, Chivas, Martell, Mumm); true comps are Diageo (DEO — Johnny Walker/Guinness/Tanqueray), Brown-Forman (BF.B — Jack Daniel's/Woodford Reserve), Remy Cointreau (RCO.PA — cognac/champagne specialist), LVMH Wines & Spirits segment (LVMH), Heineken (HEIO.AS — beer/cider adjacent), Constellation Brands (STZ — Corona/Modelo in US/wine), and Boston Beer (SAM — in-universe, craft/hard cider).

SAM.relative_comps: SAM (Boston Beer — Samuel Adams, Truly, Dogfish Head) trades at p_fcf ~8.1x with a graham_revised MoS of 0.76 and iv15 MoS of 0.77; revenue growth is modest (+3.7% YoY, flat 3Y CAGR at -0.05%), gross margin 45.6% is respectable for craft/hard-cider but net margin is thin at 5.2% and ROIC only 5.5%. The p_fcf at ~8x compares favorably vs. Constellation Brands (STZ, p_fcf ~12-14x), Molson Coors (TAP, p_fcf ~8-10x), and AB InBev (BUD, p_fcf ~14-16x), suggesting SAM is at the cheap end of the peer set. However, SAM faces structural headwinds: Truly Hard Seltzer's market-share loss to competitors (Mark Anthony Brands' White Claw, etc.) and a weakening craft-premium on the Samuel Adams franchise are genuine secular challenges, not merely cyclical. The MoS from Graham Revised reflects historical earnings power, not current trajectory — EPS recovery (+95% YoY) is largely from cost cuts/write-down comparisons. SAM is cheap-to-in-line but the cheapness is partly justified by loss of brand momentum vs. diversified peers.
SAM.rationale: Boston Beer is a US craft brewer and hard-seltzer producer (Samuel Adams, Truly, Dogfish Head, Angry Orchard, Twisted Tea); true comps are Constellation Brands (STZ — Corona/Modelo/Robert Mondavi), Molson Coors (TAP — Coors Light/Blue Moon/Carling), AB InBev (BUD — Bud Light/Stella/Corona globally), Heineken (HEIO.AS — Heineken/Dos Equis), Ambev (ABEV — Brazilian/LatAm beer), Pernod Ricard (RI.PA — in-universe, spirits premium), and Nomad Foods (NOMD — European frozen, same shelf-space competition dynamic in convenience).

SMPL.relative_comps: SMPL (Simply Good Foods — Quest Nutrition, Atkins) trades at p_fcf ~7.7x with DCF MoS 0.51 and EPV MoS 0.28; revenue grew +9% YoY with a healthy 7.5% 3Y CAGR, but EPS fell -26% YoY and ROIC is only 6.8% — significantly below BRBR (36.7%) which operates in the same branded-protein space. Vs. BRBR (p_fcf 4.4x, ROIC 36.7%) and Post Holdings (POST, p_fcf ~14x), SMPL is in the middle of the field: cheaper than POST but far more expensive than BRBR on a quality-adjusted basis. SMPL's core Atkins brand is in secular decline offset by Quest's growth, creating a portfolio in transition; the discount vs. POST reflects SMPL's lack of diversification and higher protein-supplement cyclicality. The 7.7x FCF is modestly cheap vs. the mid-cap packaged-food universe (typically 12-16x) and the MoS methods confirm this, but SMPL's ROIC relative to BRBR argues it should trade at a steeper discount — the gap is thus in-line rather than compelling cheap.
SMPL.rationale: Simply Good Foods is a branded active-nutrition company (Quest protein bars/chips, Atkins meal-replacement/snacks); true comps are BellRing Brands (BRBR — Premier Protein/Dymatize, in-universe), Post Holdings (POST — diversified branded food, owns Premier Nutrition legacy), Nomad Foods (NOMD — European frozen, similar M&A-driven branded model), Freshpet (FRPT — premium/natural, consumer engagement), Vital Farms (VITL — premium protein adjacent), Lancaster Colony (LANC — premium branded condiments/sauces), and Hain Celestial (HAIN — natural/organic packaged food, structurally similar M&A portfolio).

SPB.relative_comps: SPB (Spectrum Brands — HHI hardware, pet, home/garden, personal care) trades at p_fcf ~12.7x with an extraordinary graham_revised MoS of 0.95 and owner_earnings MoS of 0.77 — the richest method-convergence MoS in the Consumer Defensive cohort; yet revenue is declining (-5.2% YoY, -3.6% 3Y CAGR), net margin is thin (3.6%), and ROIC is near-zero (0.23%), reflecting ongoing portfolio divestitures and integration costs. Against Church & Dwight (CHD, p_fcf ~22-26x, net margin 13%, ROIC ~14%), Energizer Holdings (ENR, p_fcf ~8-10x, net margin 7%), and Newell Brands (NWL, p_fcf ~6-8x, net margin 3-5%), SPB is cheap on reported FCF but the zero ROIC signals capital is being misallocated. Crucially, proximity_52wk = 85.4% — SPB is near its 52-week high, the strongest technical position in the cohort, which conflicts with the deep value MoS and suggests either that the MoS is stale/distorted by divestitures or that the market is pricing in sum-of-parts asset value. The gap vs. CHD is justified (SPB lacks CHD's brand power and recurring R&D), but SPB is modestly cheap vs. NWL/ENR on reported FCF despite a more complex corporate structure.
SPB.rationale: Spectrum Brands is a multi-category consumer products company (HHI hardware/home improvement, Rayovac/Remington personal care, Cutter/Spectracide home/garden, pet nutrition via 8in1/Dingo); true comps are Church & Dwight (CHD — Arm & Hammer/OxiClean/Waterpik), Energizer Holdings (ENR — Energizer/Rayovac batteries), Clorox (CLX — cleaning/personal care brands), Procter & Gamble (PG — global HPC benchmark), Newell Brands (NWL — Coleman/Rubbermaid/Yankee Candle, similarly distressed multi-category), HC2/HPC adjacents, Sherwin-Williams (SHW — home improvement supply chain), and BRBR (in-universe) as a high-ROIC consumer brand contrast.

UVV.relative_comps: UVV (Universal Corporation — tobacco leaf merchant/processor) is a B2B leaf-tobacco supplier to all major cigarette manufacturers, not a branded cigarette company, which makes it fundamentally different from PM/MO/BTI and closer to an agricultural commodity processor. At p_fcf ~16.9x, UVV is expensive relative to branded tobacco (Philip Morris PM at p_fcf ~14-16x, Altria MO at ~10x, BAT/BTI at ~8x) and its own historical range (typically 8-12x), while delivering near-zero net margin (1.1%), negative EPS trend (-65.6% YoY), and ROIC of 6.0% — all below cost of capital. The EPV MoS of 0.36 and DCF MoS of 0.21 provide some support but the MoS on owner-earnings is negative (-0.31), and the EPV estimate likely captures UVV's asset-heavy leaf inventory as a floor. Universal's moat is its oligopoly position as one of three global leaf merchants (alongside Alliance One/Standard Commercial, now part of Pyxus, and DIMON); but declining global cigarette volumes are an existential headwind and the business has limited pricing power vs. its own customers (PM, MO, BAT). UVV screens rich vs. branded tobacco on FCF yield and rich vs. its own history, with momentum neutral (proximity_52wk 36.6%).
UVV.rationale: Universal Corporation is a global tobacco leaf merchant that buys, processes, and resells flue-cured and burley tobacco to cigarette manufacturers; true comps are Philip Morris International (PM — branded cigarettes/iQOS), Altria (MO — Marlboro in US/Juul), British American Tobacco (BTI — Lucky Strike/Vuse), Imperial Brands (IMPT.L — European branded), Swedish Match (SWMA.ST — snus/ZYN), Scandinavian Tobacco Group (STG.CO — cigars/pipe), Pyxus International (DIMON private predecessor — direct B2B leaf competitor), and Lamb Weston (LW — in-universe, agricultural processing peer on capital-intensity comparison).

CMCSA.relative_comps: CMCSA trades at p_fcf ~4x — the cheapest in its cable/media peer set by a wide margin: Charter (CHTR) ~9x, T/VZ ~6-8x, Disney/WBD significantly higher. Its multi-method MoS is exceptional (graham 0.90, iv15 0.86, owner_earnings 0.72), gross margin 71.7% and net 16.2% are on par with large-cap cable quality. The discount reflects cord-cutting headwinds and flat revenue (yoy -0.02%), but ROIC of 6.8% and EPS growth +30% yoy signal the FCF engine is intact; the stock hugs a 52-week low (proximity 3.9%), deepening the gap vs peers. The valuation gap vs Charter and AT&T is not fully justified by growth or quality differentials — CMCSA screens as materially cheap relative to its true peer set.
CMCSA.rationale: CMCSA is a US integrated cable/broadband/media conglomerate; true peers are the other large US cable operators (CHTR), US telcos with similar broadband/video bundles (T, VZ, TMUS), and legacy media with streaming exposure (DIS, PARA, WBD, NFLX). Industry analysts and M&A comparables consistently use this set for EV/EBITDA and FCF yield benchmarking.

FNTN.DE.relative_comps: Freenet (FNTN.DE) is a German MVNO/digital lifestyle provider — no own network — with p_fcf ~7.5x, comfortably below network-owning peers Deutsche Telekom (~13x) and Orange (~9x); MoS breadth is solid (iv15 0.79, graham 0.58) with net margin 11.1% and ROIC 11.0%, both above most European telcos. Revenue is marginally declining (-1.5% yoy, -1.5% 3y CAGR) reflecting maturing German mobile market, similar to KPN and Tele2 trajectories. The valuation discount vs network owners is partially justified by MVNO structural inferiority (no infrastructure moat), but the FCF conversion and yield are genuinely superior; vs asset-light MVNO comps the stock is fairly valued to cheap.
FNTN.DE.rationale: Freenet is Germany's largest MVNO plus a stake in Sunrise (Swiss) and Exaring/waipu.tv OTT; closest peers are network-owning German/European telcos (Deutsche Telekom, Orange, Telefonica) for earnings multiple benchmarking, and pure MVNO/MVNE operators (Tele2, KPN) for capital-intensity comparison. The MVNO business model means gross margins are lower (25.9%) than network operators but FCF conversion is higher — peer selection must account for both.

MMT.PA.relative_comps: M6 Group (MMT.PA) is a French free-to-air/pay-TV broadcaster trading at p_fcf ~10.6x, slightly below TF1 (TFI.PA at ~13.5x within universe) and broadly in line with ITV and RTL Group (~9-11x); MoS is multi-method positive (dcf 0.57, owner_earnings 0.61) but gross margin (26%) and net margin (9.8%) reflect the high-cost broadcast model. Revenue is declining (-4.3% yoy, -2.6% 3y CAGR) in line with the structural FTA advertising market contraction seen across all European commercial broadcasters. ROIC of 12.9% is the group's strongest point, above ITV (~8%) and RTL (~10%). At current price (~18% off 52wk high), MMT trades in-line with peers — a small valuation advantage over TFI within French broadcasting, but no sector re-rating catalyst is evident.
MMT.PA.rationale: M6 Group is a French commercial broadcaster (FTA + streaming); the natural peer set is other European commercial FTA broadcasters — TF1 (the in-universe direct comp), RTL Group (parent/peer), ProSieben (German analog), ITV (UK), Mediacorp, and Nordic Entertainment Group. Revenue mix (advertising, content, streaming) and capital intensity are nearly identical across this group, making them the appropriate valuation benchmark.

SAX.DE.relative_comps: Stroeer (SAX.DE) is a German out-of-home (OOH) media/digital advertising group at p_fcf ~5.7x — a significant discount to global OOH peers JCDecaux (~15x) and Clear Channel (~20x+ on recovery multiples), though those trade at premium for global scale. Vs global ad agency parents (WPP, IPG, OMC at 8-12x FCF), SAX is cheap; MoS is strong (dcf 0.59, owner_earnings 0.71), gross margin 42% is healthy for OOH, and revenue growth +1.4% yoy and +5.4% 3y CAGR are respectable in the European ad market. ROIC of 7.4% is below the ad-tech/digital peers but above pure OOH infrastructure. The discount likely reflects German market concentration risk and limited international growth optionality vs JCDecaux; nonetheless the FCF multiple gap looks excessive — SAX screens cheap on an absolute and relative basis.
SAX.DE.rationale: Stroeer's business is German-centric OOH advertising (billboards, digital screens, online performance marketing); the true peer set is the global OOH duopoly (JCDecaux, Clear Channel) for billboard/DOOH valuation, plus the holding companies (WPP, Publicis, IPG, OMC) to the extent Stroeer's digital marketing arm competes with them. Stroeer's model is higher-margin than pure agency but lower-margin than pure software — peers must span both.

TFI.PA.relative_comps: TF1 Group (TFI.PA) is France's largest commercial broadcaster at p_fcf ~13.5x — a premium to M6/MMT.PA (~10.6x) within this universe and at the high end of the European FTA peer range (ITV ~9x, RTL ~10x, ProSieben ~8x); MoS methods are positive (dcf 0.62, owner_earnings 0.63) but the higher multiple is hard to justify given near-identical structural pressures: revenue -2.5% yoy, -2.9% 3y CAGR, gross margin only 19.3% (below M6 at 26%), net margin 6.7% (below M6 at 9.8%), and ROIC 9.4% (below M6 at 12.9%). TF1's premium vs M6 appears unjustified by fundamentals — it is essentially the same declining FTA business at worse unit economics, suggesting TFI.PA is rich relative to its direct French competitor and in-line to slightly rich vs the broader European peer set.
TFI.PA.rationale: TF1 Group is France's leading FTA broadcaster, a close structural twin of M6 Group (MMT.PA) with the same advertising revenue cyclicality, content spend structure, and streaming-disruption headwinds. The broader peer set is European commercial FTA/pay broadcasters: M6, RTL Group, ProSieben, ITV, and Nordic Entertainment. Given TF1's worse margin profile and similar growth trajectory vs M6, the ~27% FCF multiple premium to M6 is the key mispricing signal.

VEON.relative_comps: VEON is an emerging-market multi-country mobile telco (Ukraine, Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan, Pakistan, Bangladesh) at p_fcf ~5.85x with the highest single-method MoS in the group (dcf 0.74), but near-zero Altman-Z (0.57) flags meaningful financial distress risk given high leverage across EM geographies. Revenue growth +11.8% yoy and +6.0% 3y CAGR is well above EM peers MTS (Russia, ~4%), Telkomsel (Indonesia) and DIGI (Malaysia); gross margin 69.8% is genuine telco quality. However, ROIC 8.8% is dragged by debt-laden balance sheets typical of EM telcos, and the low Altman-Z coupled with geopolitical concentration (Ukraine) creates a binary risk profile most peers do not carry. The optically cheap FCF multiple is therefore partially justified by distress and political risk, not a clean value discount — in-line to marginally cheap on an apples-to-apples EM-risk-adjusted basis.
VEON.rationale: VEON is a pure EM mobile operator; true peers are other frontier/emerging-market telcos — MTS (Russia), MTSS, Beeline-branded operators in Central Asia, Indonesian telcos (Telkomsel, XL Axiata), Malaysian DIGI, and Pakistan's PTCL. The common thread is single or multi-market EM mobile with high capex intensity, currency risk, and regulatory exposure. Comparing VEON to developed-market telcos (CMCSA, FNTN) would misstate the risk-adjusted discount.

YELP.relative_comps: Yelp (YELP) is a local-search/review-to-lead-gen platform at p_fcf ~4.7x — the cheapest in the local advertising technology space: TripAdvisor ~8x, Angi ~6-7x, and Google/Meta (the dominant local ad competitors) at 20-30x+. MoS is broadly positive (dcf 0.66, iv15 0.86, graham 0.44) and the 90.3% gross margin reflects the software/marketplace nature of the business. Revenue growth is sluggish (+3.7% yoy, +7% 3y CAGR) and net margin 9.9% / ROIC 9.0% are modest vs the platform's gross margin potential, reflecting heavy S&M spend. The extreme valuation discount to Google/Meta is structurally rational (they own local search) but versus direct local-ad platform peers (TRIP, ANGI), Yelp's FCF multiple and MoS breadth indicate genuine cheapness, particularly given the self-service advertising shift and buyback-driven EPS improvement.
YELP.rationale: Yelp monetizes local business reviews via cost-per-click and lead-gen advertising — the true peer set is local digital advertising platforms and review aggregators: TripAdvisor (TRIP), Angi (ANGI), IAC (which owns various digital properties), and Google/Meta as the structural competitive threat. The valuation gap vs TRIP and ANGI (both similarly subscale vs Big Tech) is the most informative relative signal.

ACX.MC.relative_comps: ACX.MC (Acerinox, stainless-steel flat products) is in-line to mildly rich vs true European stainless peers Outokumpu (OUT1V.HE) and Aperam (APERAM.AS): p_fcf of 24.5x compares poorly to Outokumpu's typical 6-10x and Aperam's ~8-12x trough-to-mid-cycle range, and net margin is actually negative (-0.7%) on a revenue base that has contracted -13% over 3 years, so there is no FCF quality behind the p_fcf. On the positive side, the stock is trading near its 52-week high (proximity 0.93), suggesting the market has already re-rated on a recovery narrative; gross margin of 18% is typical for commodity stainless converters but ROIC of 6.5% is below cost of capital. The premium vs peers is not justified by growth or quality — it reflects sector momentum and a cyclical-recovery trade, making the current valuation rich vs fundamentals.
ACX.MC.rationale: Acerinox is a pure-play European stainless-steel flat-products producer whose real comparables are Outokumpu (Finland, largest European stainless producer), Aperam (Luxembourg/Brazil, stainless/specialty steel), and North American mini-mill steel peers NUE/STLD for relative cycle valuation. ArcelorMittal (MT) and Hyundai Steel (005380.KS) form the broader carbon-steel universe. The company's economics — thin margins, commodity pricing, high capital intensity, volume leverage to European industrial demand — are identical to these peers.

BCC.relative_comps: BCC (Boise Cascade) spans two businesses — engineered wood products (EWP: LVL, I-joists) and building materials distribution — making its true peers a mix of Weyerhaeuser (WY) and LP Building Solutions (LPX) on the manufacturing side and BlueLinx (BXC) / UFP Technologies (UFPI) on the distribution side; TREX and EXP (Eagle Materials) add cross-sector building products context. The p_fcf of ~198x is misleadingly high because FY2025 FCF collapsed with lumber/OSB price normalization (eps_yoy -63%, revenue_cagr_3y -8.6%), while the DCF MoS of +0.45 and owner-earnings MoS of +0.41 argue trough normalization makes it cheap on mid-cycle earnings. ROIC of 20.8% is genuinely superior to peers (WY ~10-12%, LPX ~15%) and reflects BCC's asset-light distribution arm. Trading near 52wk low (proximity 0.10) while ROIC suggests mid-cycle earning power is compelling — the gap is partly justified by commodity cycle headwinds but appears wider than warranted.
BCC.rationale: Boise Cascade is fundamentally a building-products manufacturer (engineered wood panels) plus a large-format building materials distribution network serving US residential and light commercial construction. True structural peers are LP Building Solutions (LPX) for OSB/EWP manufacturing, Weyerhaeuser (WY) for forest-products context, UFP Industries (UFPI) for value-added wood distribution, and BlueLinx (BXC) for commodity distribution. BLDR and GMS add homebuilder-supply-chain context. Eagle Materials (EXP) and Summit Materials (SUM) reflect the broader US building-materials cycle.

KEMIRA.HE.relative_comps: Kemira is a niche specialty-chemical company serving water treatment, paper/pulp, and oil-field applications — its closest listed comparables are Ecolab (ECL) for the water-treatment angle, Nouryon (private) and Clariant (CLN.SW) for paper-process chemicals, and LANXESS (LXS.DE) / BASF for broader European specialty chemicals context. At p_fcf of 14.1x and dcf_mos of +0.32, Kemira screens as in-line to slightly cheap vs Ecolab (p_fcf typically 25-35x) and modestly cheap vs European specialty peers (LANXESS ~10-13x, Clariant ~15-18x), but the negative revenue trend (-6.6% YoY, -8.3% 3yr CAGR) and EPS decline (-27%) weigh against a re-rating. Net margin of 6.6% and ROIC of 8.1% are below ECL's quality profile (ROIC ~15%+), so the discount is partially justified by lower-quality capital returns; the stock near its 52-week low (proximity 0.20) suggests the market is not yet pricing a recovery.
KEMIRA.HE.rationale: Kemira (Helsinki) is a B2B specialty chemicals supplier focused on process and functional chemicals for the water, pulp & paper, and oil & gas industries. The business model — long-term supply contracts, formulated chemistry, technical service — makes it most comparable to Ecolab (ECL, water/hygiene treatment chemicals), SNF Group (private, polyacrylamide for water), Nouryon (private, paper process chemicals), and the relevant specialty-chemicals divisions of BASF, LANXESS, and Clariant. Albemarle (ALB) provides a benchmark for specialty-chemical cycle valuation.

SSRM.relative_comps: SSR Mining is a mid-tier gold/silver producer (Copler in Turkey, Seabee in Canada, Puna in Argentina) operating after the 2024 Copler heap-leach pad collapse; true peers are Kinross (KGC), Eldorado Gold (EGO), and Pan American Silver (PAAS) for size/geographic mix, plus B2Gold (BTG) and Equinox Gold for all-in-sustaining-cost (AISC) profile comparison. At p_fcf of 23.6x and graham_revised_mos of +0.79, SSRM screens as cheap on a recovery basis — revenue surged +66.5% as Copler is being rebuilt/restarted and Seabee/Puna carried production — but ROIC of only 2.2% reflects the capital drag of the Turkey remediation. Compared to KGC (~15x p_fcf) and BTG (~10-12x), SSRM's FCF multiple looks in-line accounting for reduced production; the large graham MoS argues intrinsic value is substantially above market, suggesting cheap vs peers if Copler restart materializes.
SSRM.rationale: SSR Mining's peer set is defined by its asset profile — mid-tier, multi-jurisdiction gold/silver production with above-average geopolitical risk (Turkey, Argentina). The closest comparables by asset mix, AISC, and market cap are Kinross Gold (KGC), Eldorado Gold (EGO), and Pan American Silver (PAAS) for Latin/EM-jurisdiction overlay; B2Gold (BTG) for West Africa/Americas mid-tier; and Hecla Mining (HL) / First Majestic (AG) for silver-leverage comparison. Agnico Eagle (AEM) and Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) serve as quality anchors for the sector valuation range.

7352.T.relative_comps: TWOSTONE&Sons (Midworks IT engineer matching + digital marketing) trades at a trailing PE of ~48x and P/B of ~36x, steep even against Japan's high-growth HR-tech peers: Visional/BizReach (4393.T) trades at ~40-50x forward earnings but is larger and more diversified; Recruit Holdings (6098.T) commands ~35-40x on vastly superior scale and global monetisation; Meitec (9744.T) and Pasona (2168.T) sit at 20-30x as more traditional staffing plays with lower structural growth. TWOSTONE's ~33% revenue CAGR justifies a growth premium but its micro-cap scale, single-market concentration and freelance-platform model leave it dependent on Japan's IT labour tightness cycle; the PE gap vs Recruit is unjustified by quality — Recruit has higher ROIC and a proven international business — so TWOSTONE is rich on absolute terms, broadly in-line with pure Japan HR-tech growth proxies given its growth rate, but offers no margin of safety at current prices.
7352.T.rationale: True peers are Japanese HR-tech and IT staffing platforms (Visional/BizReach, Recruit Holdings, Meitec, Pasona, Modis/Adecco Japan) sharing the same end-market of matching IT talent to enterprise demand in Japan's structurally tight labour market. TWOSTONE is a sub-scale pure-play on the freelance IT engineer segment; its matching platform model and digital-media flywheel are most directly comparable to Visional's BizReach and Recruit's Indeed/Glassdoor Japan operations.

DNR.MI.relative_comps: Industrie De Nora trades at EV/EBITDA ~9x and PE ~15-19x — a significant discount to green-hydrogen pure-plays: Nel ASA (NEL.OL) is loss-making and trades on revenue multiples >5x EV/sales; thyssenkrupp nucera (NCR.DE) trades at EV/EBITDA of 25-40x on thin margins; Plug Power (PLUG) is deep in losses and commands a speculative premium. De Nora's discount is partly justified because it is an electrodes/components supplier rather than a systems integrator, giving lower headline growth optics but far superior margins (gross ~37%, EBITDA margin ~20%) and positive free cash flow — qualities absent in most pure-play electrolysis peers. Against Siemens Energy (ENR.DE), which is diversified and profitable, De Nora looks cheap given its green-hydrogen torque; analyst consensus targets ~EUR 10 vs the ~EUR 6.85 share price, suggesting genuine mispricing driven by green-hydrogen market uncertainty overhang rather than fundamental deterioration.
DNR.MI.rationale: True peers span the electrochemical value chain: electrode and coating suppliers (De Nora's core), alkaline and PEM electrolyzer system builders (Nel, thyssenkrupp nucera, ITM Power), and diversified energy-transition industrials (Siemens Energy). The relevant comparison set is companies that monetise industrial electrochemistry and the hydrogen economy — not generic Italian industrials. De Nora is uniquely profitable among this peer set, making it structurally undervalued relative to the growth-story multiples carried by loss-making pure-plays in the same thematic bucket.
