RWAY.MI.relative_comps: Rai Way trades at EV/EBITDA ~8.5x versus INWIT (Italy's largest independent tower company) at ~11.9x — a 28% discount despite near-identical country risk, similar tower/infrastructure asset mix, and comparable EBITDA margins. The discount is only partially justified: Rai Way is 65% owned by RAI (state broadcaster), limiting strategic optionality and free-float, and its broadcast-tower client base is structurally more concentrated and at risk from streaming-driven linear TV decline than INWIT's MNO tenants. However, ongoing merger talks with EI Towers could crystallise value — a combined entity would trade closer to INWIT multiples and Cellnex (CLNX.MC, ~14-16x EV/EBITDA) given scale efficiencies; at current prices RWAY appears cheap relative to INWIT and to European tower peers, with the discount exceeding what the RAI ownership overhang and broadcast-mix risk rationally warrant.
RWAY.MI.rationale: True peers are broadcast and telecom tower/infrastructure operators sharing the towerco business model: long-term lease contracts, high recurring EBITDA, asset-backed capital intensity. INWIT is the most direct Italian peer (listed, comparable regulatory environment); Cellnex is the European towerco benchmark. American Tower and SBA Communications set the global valuation ceiling. EI Towers (privately held, F2i/MFE-MediaForEurope) is the closest operational peer given its Italian broadcast-tower focus — the proposed merger between RWAY and EI Towers is the clearest signal that these two are the natural comparables in the Italian broadcast infrastructure market.

A2A.MI.relative_comps: A2A trades at a p_fcf of 6.9x — strikingly cheap versus the European multi-utility sector norm of 15–25x FCF and cheap even relative to close peer Hera (HER.MI at 26.4x p_fcf) and Iren; the multi-method MoS confirms deep value with graham_revised_mos of 0.83 and iv15_deep_value_mos of 0.49, though owner_earnings_mos is thin at 0.18. On growth, A2A shows a positive one-year revenue rebound (+9.3%) but the 3yr CAGR is a negative 15.7%, putting it behind growth-oriented peers like Veolia or E.ON's transition-utility segment; EPS is down 14% yoy, consistent with European utility sector margin compression on energy price normalisation. ROIC of 5.5% and net margin of 5.5% are in line with regulated-utility peers; gross margin of 9.8% sits above Hera's 5.7%, reflecting A2A's relatively better generation/waste mix. Price is in the lower quarter of its 52-week range and below most peers' recent momentum — the discount appears only partially justified by the EPS decline and 3yr revenue drag; the core regulated asset base and waste/environment exposure are not structurally impaired.
A2A.MI.rationale: True peers are Italian multi-utilities (Hera, Iren, ACEA) plus pan-European integrated utilities (E.ON, RWE, Veolia, ENEL) sharing regulated distribution networks, captive end-markets, and high capital intensity in electricity, gas, water, and waste. A2A's p_fcf of 6.9x is a substantial discount to all of these on the key earnings-yield and asset-replacement metrics, with Graham-revised MoS of 0.83 suggesting the market is pricing in permanent impairment that the regulated asset book does not warrant.

HER.MI.relative_comps: Hera trades at p_fcf of 26.4x — materially richer than A2A.MI (6.9x) and broadly in line with regulated Italian utility comps such as Italgas and Snam, which historically carry premium multiples for their pure-play gas distribution concessions; the graham_revised_mos of 0.79 and iv15_deep_value_mos of 0.57 signal theoretical intrinsic-value support, but the owner_earnings methodology returns no valid result, suggesting FCF quality may be thin when maintenance capex is fully deducted. Revenue has declined 1.9% yoy and 13.6% on a 3yr CAGR, the weakest top-line trend among Italian multi-utilities, while EPS fell 5.9%; gross margin of 5.7% and net margin of 3.6% are both below A2A's equivalent figures, pointing to a less profitable service/geography mix. Price sits in the lower third of the 52-week range with momentum slightly below sma200 — the premium p_fcf multiple relative to A2A is only partially justified by Hera's more stable regulated gas-distribution exposure; the growth and margin disadvantage make the gap hard to defend fully.
HER.MI.rationale: Hera's true peer set is the Italian multi-utility cohort — A2A, Iren, ACEA, Italgas, Snam — plus European waste-and-water operators like Veolia and Suez/Veolia-combined. Within this group Hera commands a p_fcf premium to A2A and Iren despite worse revenue trajectory, thinner margins, and no validated owner-earnings figure, making it relatively rich; the Graham-based MoS offers comfort on book-value grounds but cannot offset the FCF-quality concern for a capital-intensive regulated operator.

VOW3.DE.relative_comps: VOW3.DE trades at the deepest discount in its true OEM peer set: p_fcf is undefined (currently FCF-negative due to EV investment surge and margin compression), yet graham_revised_mos of 0.59 and owner_earnings_mos of 0.80 indicate book and normalised earnings power well above market price — a profile matched only by BMW.DE (owner_mos 0.54) and MBG.DE (owner_mos 0.79) in-universe, with MBG roughly equivalent on normalised earnings but carrying slightly better margins (net 3.9% vs VOW's 2.3%) and a higher altman_z (1.31 vs 0.96). Out-of-universe, Stellantis (STLA) and Renault trade at 3-5x earnings with similar single-digit net margins, Toyota/Honda at 8-10x with far superior ROIC (~12-15%), and Ford/GM at 5-7x P/E with comparable leverage risk; VOW3 at a residual P/BV below 0.3x is the cheapest on book-value across this group. The discount is only partly justified: Altman-Z of 0.964 (genuine distress territory), eps_yoy of -38%, and a deeply negative momentum print (price ~20% off 52-week low, trading below SMA200) reflect real fundamental deterioration — Dieselgate legacy costs, EV transition capex, China market share erosion, and labour-cost rigidities in Germany that peers without those union agreements do not face. The gap vs Toyota/Honda is fully justified by quality and ROIC; the gap vs BMW and MBG is about 30-40% wider than warranted by comparable balance-sheet risk, suggesting partial mispricing rather than a pure value trap.
VOW3.DE.rationale: True peers are the global mass-market/premium OEM oligopoly: BMW.DE and MBG.DE (German rivals with identical tariff and union exposure), STLA and Renault (European volume OEMs with overlapping end-markets and similar capex cycles), and Toyota/Honda/GM/Ford (global benchmarks for scale, ROIC and EV transition spend). PAH3.DE is excluded as a pure holding-company overlay. On every book-value and normalised-earnings metric VOW3 screens cheapest in this group; momentum, altman-z, and collapsing net margins confirm the discount is real but also that risk is elevated — making it cheap-vs-peers with meaningful fundamental caveats rather than a clean mispricing.

3969.HK.relative_comps: 3969.HK (China Yida Rail) screens on acquirers_multiple only and carries p_fcf=0 (FCF not cleanly observable), EPV-MoS of ~80% is the lone positive signal, but Altman-Z of 1.45 sits in distress territory and 3-yr revenue CAGR is negative (-4.9%). Versus Class I US rails (CSX, NSC, UNP — typically 18-28x FCF, 25-35% margins, investment-grade) and CN Rail / CP (premium growth multiples), 3969.HK trades at a structural discount driven by Chinese regulatory opacity, lower scale, and balance-sheet fragility rather than any identifiable value creation. Momentum is poor: proximity to 52-wk high is only 23% and the stock trails SMA200.
3969.HK.rationale: True peers are large-cap freight-rail operators globally (CSX, NSC, UNP, CP, CNI) and rail-equipment specialists (WAB, DSV). Discount reflects real risks (Altman-Z distress zone, negative revenue CAGR, FCF opacity in Chinese holding-company structure) rather than simple undervaluation; the apparent cheapness is a quality/governance discount, not a pure mispricing.

ALG.relative_comps: ALG (Alamo Group — roadside and vegetation mowing equipment) trades at p_fcf of 12.3x and EPV-MoS just barely negative (-6%), with DCF-MoS of +31% and owner-earnings MoS of +5%, suggesting mild undervaluation versus intrinsic value. Revenue is essentially flat (-1.5% YoY, +2% 3-yr CAGR) and EPS dipped -11%; gross margin of 25% and net margin of 6.5% are below AGCO (28-30% gross) and far below Deere's equipment segment (35%+). ROIC of 9.8% is respectable but not exceptional. Price sits at the 52-wk low vicinity (proximity 4%), well below SMA200 — momentum is poor. Against AGCO (depressed but larger-scale) and CNHI, ALG screens as in-line to modestly cheap given its niche mowing/infrastructure focus and stable municipal end-market, but growth is structurally slow.
ALG.rationale: Closest true peers are specialty agricultural and municipal-equipment OEMs: AGCO, CNH Industrial (CNHI), Deere's compact/construction segment, LNN (irrigation), and European niche players like Bucher Industries. ALG's specialty government/municipal end-market provides recession resilience that mainstream ag peers lack, partially justifying a modest premium; current discount to intrinsic value at near-52wk lows appears real.

AOS.relative_comps: A.O. Smith (water heaters, water treatment) trades at p_fcf of 14.7x — in line with sector mid-caps — with convergence across DCF (+20%), graham (+67%), owner-earnings (+22%), and IV15 (+61%) MoS signals, pointing to genuine undervaluation. Revenue growth is near-zero (+0.3% YoY, +0.7% CAGR), but ROIC of 24% is outstanding for an industrial manufacturer, gross margin of 39% is premium-tier, and net margin of 14% leads the group. Versus Watts Water (WTS, ~20x FCF), Bradford White (private), and Rinnai (private), AOS screens cheap on p_fcf yet superior on quality metrics. Price is only 11% from the 52-wk low and below SMA200, so technical momentum is negative — but fundamentals justify at least in-line valuation.
AOS.rationale: True peers are water-heating and water-treatment equipment makers: Watts Water (WTS), Bradford White, Rinnai (private), Rheem (private), and tangentially GNRC for residential utility infrastructure. AOS's 24% ROIC and 39% gross margin are best-in-class for the category; the current 52-wk-low vicinity pricing combined with multi-method MoS signals makes this a genuine quality-at-discount situation.

AXON.relative_comps: AXON Enterprise (Tasers, body cameras, cloud software for law enforcement) trades at p_fcf of 647x with zero MoS across all value methods — a pure growth/momentum instrument. Revenue CAGR of 33% and recurring SaaS software penetration justify the premium in a growth-stock framework, but EPS declined -69% YoY (heavy investment phase) and ROIC is effectively zero (0.8%). Against law-enforcement-tech peers (Motorola Solutions at ~30x earnings, L3Harris at ~20x), AXON is priced at a massive premium; the SaaS law-enforcement cloud has near-zero direct listed competition, but the valuation embeds a decade of perfect execution. Proximity to 52-wk high is only 27%, meaning momentum has rolled over despite the growth story.
AXON.rationale: True peers are public-safety technology and communications companies: Motorola Solutions (MSI), L3Harris (LHX), and niche players like Wrap Technologies (WRAP). AXON's software ecosystem moat is real and differentiated, but the valuation is pricing in blue-sky outcomes; the momentum rollback and negative ROIC make this rich even accounting for SaaS optionality.

CMRE.relative_comps: Costamare (container-ship and dry-bulk lessor) trades at p_fcf of 4.1x — among the cheapest in the universe — with DCF-MoS of 38% and owner-earnings MoS of 60%, flagging material undervaluation. However, revenue cratered -58% YoY (normalization of pandemic-era container rates) with a 3-yr CAGR of -8%, though EPS still grew +17% suggesting cost discipline. Gross margin of 57% and net margin of 42% are exceptionally high for shipping, reflecting the asset-light lessor model versus pure liner operators. Versus Global Ship Lease (GSL, ~3-4x FCF), Safe Bulkers (SB, ~4-6x), and Star Bulk (SBLK, ~5-7x), CMRE is in-line to marginally cheap, but the sector consensus is that earnings are cyclically elevated and normalizing. Momentum is positive: 78% of 52-wk range and above SMA200.
CMRE.rationale: Real peers are container-ship and dry-bulk lessors and owners: Global Ship Lease (GSL), Danaos (DAC), Diana Shipping (DSX), Star Bulk (SBLK), and ZIM. The lessor model (fixed-rate long-term charters) insulates CMRE more than pure liners; the 4x FCF and 60% owner-earnings MoS with positive momentum represents genuine cheap-vs-peers status after rate normalization is priced in.

GBX.relative_comps: Greenbrier Companies (railcar manufacturing and leasing) has p_fcf=0 (negative or irregular FCF in manufacturing cycle), graham_revised MoS of 72% is the main signal but EPV and owner-earnings are both negative, indicating earnings quality issues. Revenue fell -9% YoY and gross margin of 19% is thin. Versus Trinity Industries (TRN, ~8-10x normalized FCF) and FreightCar America, GBX looks optically cheap on graham-book but weak on cash generation. Momentum is fair (41% of 52-wk range, above SMA200). The railcar industry is cyclical and capital-intensive; thin margins and irregular FCF warrant caution.
GBX.rationale: True peers are North American railcar manufacturers and lessors: Trinity Industries (TRN), FreightCar America (RAIL), American Railcar Industries (ARII), and leasing arms of GATX. GBX's dual manufacturing-plus-leasing model should command a slight premium to pure manufacturers, but FCF irregularity and negative operating leverage in the down cycle place it in-line rather than demonstrably cheap.

GE.relative_comps: GE Aerospace (post-Vernova spin, pure jet-engine and services) trades at p_fcf of 48x with no MoS on any method — a premium growth industrial. Revenue grew +18% YoY with 16% 3-yr CAGR driven by LEAP engine deliveries and services revenue; gross margin of 37% and net margin of 19% are strong for an OEM, though ROIC of only 4% reflects the capital-intensive nature and legacy pension/debt. Against RTX Pratt & Whitney (~25-30x earnings) and Safran (~22x), GE is priced at a premium that embeds services (aftermarket) growth — justified but not cheap. Momentum is excellent: 82% of 52-wk range, well above SMA200.
GE.rationale: True peers are large-scale jet-engine and aerospace OEMs and their aftermarket specialists: RTX/Pratt & Whitney, Safran (SAF.PA), Rolls-Royce (RR.L), MTU Aero (MTU.DE), and pure-play aftermarket specialists HEI and TDG. GE's premium to RTX and Safran is partly justified by faster engine-delivery ramp and higher services mix, but no MoS on any method confirms it is priced for perfection.

HEI.relative_comps: HEICO (FAA-approved aircraft PMA parts and electronics) trades at p_fcf of 54x — a premium multiple for a premium business. Revenue CAGR of 27% (3yr) and EPS growth of +34% are exceptional; gross margin of 40% and net margin of 15% are strong, ROIC of 10% modest but improving. No MoS on any method — price is ahead of all valuation anchors. Versus TransDigm (TDG, ~25-30x EBITDA, near-monopoly pricing power) and RTX's aftermarket, HEI has historically traded at a valuation premium for its ethical reputation and organic growth. At 54x FCF with 71% of 52-wk range, momentum is healthy but valuation is stretched.
HEI.rationale: True peers are defense and commercial-aviation aftermarket specialists: TransDigm (TDG), Ducommun (DCO), Kaman Aerospace, and RTX's Collins aftermarket. HEI's distribution-based moat and FAA-PMA monopoly in niche parts warrant a premium, but 54x FCF puts it in rich territory even against TDG. The gap is partially justified by quality/growth but the absolute valuation leaves little room for error.

HUBG.relative_comps: Hub Group (intermodal and trucking logistics) trades at p_fcf of 18.3x with DCF MoS of 17% and owner-earnings MoS of 23% — modest undervaluation versus intrinsic. Revenue fell -6% YoY and -2% 3-yr CAGR reflecting freight-market normalization; gross margin of 85% is high (brokerage/asset-light) but net margin compresses to 2.6% after purchased transportation costs. ROIC of 8.2% is fair. Versus C.H. Robinson (CHRW, ~15-18x FCF), J.B. Hunt (JBHT, ~20-25x), and XPO (~15-20x), HUBG appears in-line on valuation. Momentum is positive (50% of 52-wk range, above SMA200).
HUBG.rationale: True peers are North American intermodal and truck brokerage operators: C.H. Robinson (CHRW), J.B. Hunt Transport (JBHT), Echo Global Logistics (ECHO), XPO, and Forward Air (FWRD). HUBG's intermodal-heavy mix positions it between asset-light brokers and asset-heavy carriers; valuation is in-line with the group after freight normalization, with modest DCF support but no clear catalyst for re-rating.

IT.relative_comps: Gartner (IT research and advisory) at p_fcf of 10.1x is deceptively cheap on that metric, with DCF MoS of 53% and owner-earnings MoS of 49% — strong signals. Revenue grew +3.7% YoY and 5.9% CAGR (subscription resilience), gross margin of 68% and net margin of 11% are respectable for a human-capital-intensive advisory firm, and ROIC of 21% is excellent. However, graham_revised MoS is deeply negative (-81%) reflecting high goodwill from acquisitions. EPS fell -40% YoY (one-time items likely). Against Forrester (FORR, smaller, ~15x FCF), and Informa Analytics (listed as part of INFO.L), IT screens decidedly cheap on cash metrics; ROIC superiority is genuine. Price is only 9% from 52-wk low (poor momentum).
IT.rationale: True peers are subscription-based B2B research and advisory providers: Forrester Research (FORR), IDC (owned by IDG, private), Informa (INFO.L), and specialized tech-research platforms. Gartner's scale advantages, renewal rates >100%, and 21% ROIC are structurally superior to any listed peer; 10x FCF with 50%+ MoS on cash methods is genuine undervaluation despite the goodwill-inflated book value.

JUN3.DE.relative_comps: Jungheinrich (German forklift and warehouse logistics equipment) trades at p_fcf of 6.5x with DCF MoS of 63% and owner-earnings MoS of 72% — strong value signals in a cyclical industrial. Revenue grew only +2% YoY (slow recovery) and EPS fell -64% YoY — a sharp earnings miss — with net margin compressed to 1.9% and ROIC of 5.8%. Gross margin of 32% is mid-range for capital equipment. Altman-Z of 1.87 is borderline, and price is near the 52-wk low (0.6% proximity) in deep downtrend. Against Kion Group (KION.DE, ~8-10x normalized FCF), Toyota Industries Logistics (private majority), and Crown Equipment (private), JUN3.DE screens cheap vs peers on FCF metrics but earnings quality is currently very poor.
JUN3.DE.rationale: True peers are intralogistics and forklift equipment OEMs: Kion Group (KION.DE), Toyota Material Handling (TI.T partial), Crown Equipment (private), and Hyster-Yale (HY). JUN3.DE's 6.5x p_fcf and 70%+ owner-earnings MoS reflect deep cyclical trough pricing; the earnings compression is cycle-driven not structural, making this cheap-vs-peers on a normalized basis.

KGX.DE.relative_comps: Koenig & Bauer (printing press machinery) trades at p_fcf of 7.0x with DCF MoS of 52%, owner-earnings MoS of 60%, and IV15 MoS of 85% — one of the more convincing value setups in the group. Revenue fell -1.8% YoY with near-zero 3-yr CAGR; EPS fell -36% and net margin is only 2.0% with ROIC of 3%. Altman-Z of 1.16 is in distress territory. Price is near the 52-wk low (0.5% proximity), deeply under SMA200. The printing machinery industry faces secular decline from digital media. Versus Heidelberger Druckmaschinen (HDM.DE, private/OTC) and Manroland (private), the sector is structurally challenged. Value signals may be a value trap.
KGX.DE.rationale: True peers are commercial and industrial printing press manufacturers: Heidelberger Druckmaschinen (HDM.DE), Manroland Goss (private), Komori (6222.T), and tangentially Bobst Group (BOBST.SW). KGX.DE screens numerically cheap but the secular headwinds in print media equipment make the value signals potentially illusory; cheap-vs-peers with a value-trap caveat.

LIGHT.AS.relative_comps: Signify (formerly Philips Lighting — LED and connected lighting) is the correct parent frame; LIGHT.AS appears to be a smaller lighting or electrical components player. At p_fcf of 6.1x with DCF MoS of 60% and owner-earnings MoS of 57%, value signals are strong. Revenue fell -6% YoY and -8% 3-yr CAGR, EPS -21%, net margin of 4.4% and ROIC of 6.9% are below-average. Altman-Z of 1.4 is borderline. Price is at 58% of 52-wk range, modestly above SMA200. Against Signify (LIGHT.AS in Amsterdam, p_fcf ~8-10x historically) and Acuity Brands (AYI, ~15x FCF), this screens cheap but with structural revenue headwinds in traditional lighting (LED commoditization).
LIGHT.AS.rationale: True peers for Signify/LIGHT.AS are connected lighting and electrical-equipment companies: Acuity Brands (AYI), Legrand (LR.PA), Eaton's electrical segment (ETN), and Hubbell (HUBB). Revenue contraction from LED commoditization is the key risk; at 6x FCF with 60% DCF MoS, the discount is real but partially warranted by margin compression and market-share pressure.

MMS.relative_comps: Maximus (government services BPO — health/human services program administration) trades at p_fcf of 9.6x with graham_revised MoS of 76% and IV15 MoS of 67% — genuine value signals. Revenue grew +2.4% YoY and 5.5% 3-yr CAGR (stable government-contract base), EPS +10%, net margin of 5.9% and ROIC of 9.1% are decent for a government BPO. The business model (outsourced Medicaid enrollment, unemployment, child-support services) has high switching costs and long-duration contracts. Against SAIC (~12x FCF), Leidos (LDOS, ~15x), and ICF International (~12x), MMS screens cheap. Price is only 8% from the 52-wk low (poor momentum) but fundamental quality is solid.
MMS.rationale: True peers are government-services and health-program BPO providers: SAIC, Leidos (LDOS), ICF International (ICFI), DXC Technology, and CACI International. Maximus has the most concentrated exposure to Medicaid/welfare-program outsourcing, giving it a recession-resistant revenue stream that peers lack; 9.6x FCF is materially cheap vs LDOS at 15x with similar contract quality.

NORCO.OL.relative_comps: Norco (Norwegian construction and engineering) at p_fcf of 115.8x — an extreme outlier — with no MoS on any method except EV/gross-profit, suggests a value trap or earnings-distorted FCF. Revenue grew +9.5% YoY, EPS +29%, but net margin is only 5.7% and the sky-high p_fcf implies FCF is either negative or near zero after working capital. Against AF Gruppen (AFG.OL, ~10-15x normalized FCF), Skanska (SKANSKA.ST, ~15x), and NCC (NCC.ST, ~12x), NORCO screens very expensive on cash flow but potentially in-line on EV/EBITDA if the FCF distortion is working-capital timing. Price is near the 52-wk low (6% proximity), contradicting the expensive valuation — the market is skeptical.
NORCO.OL.rationale: True peers are Nordic and European construction and engineering contractors: AF Gruppen (AFG.OL), Skanska (SKANSKA.ST), NCC (NCC.ST), PEAB (PEAB-B.ST), and Bouygues Construction (BOUYGUES.PA). At 115x p_fcf with no MoS on traditional methods, NORCO is rich vs listed Norwegian peers even accounting for FCF timing; the combination of expensive valuation and price near 52-wk lows is a concerning signal.

PRG.relative_comps: PROG Holdings (lease-to-own retail financing platform) trades at p_fcf of 4.4x — extremely cheap — with MoS across all five methods: DCF 64%, EPV 30%, graham 78%, owner-earnings 88%, IV15 50%. This is the most multi-method-confirmed value in the Industrials group. Revenue fell -2.2% YoY and 3-yr CAGR -2.5%, EPS -21% (softening consumer), net margin of 6.1% and ROIC of 12.6% are solid for the rent-to-own sector. Versus Rent-A-Center (RCII, ~6-8x FCF) and Aaron's (AAN, ~5-8x), PRG screens cheaper with better MoS. Price at 62% of 52-wk range and above SMA200 — momentum is constructive.
PRG.rationale: True peers are lease-to-own and rent-to-own platforms: Rent-A-Center (RCII, parent of Acima), Aaron's Holdings (AAN), and tangentially Upbound Group (UPBD which is also in this universe at 4.5x p_fcf). PRG's virtual lease-to-own model (no stores, embedded in retail partners) provides higher capital efficiency than brick-and-mortar peers; 4.4x FCF with five-method MoS convergence is an unusually strong value signal.

RAND.AS.relative_comps: Randstad (global staffing and HR services) trades at p_fcf of 5.6x with DCF MoS of 61% and owner-earnings MoS of 48% — solid value in a cyclical sector. Revenue fell -4.3% YoY and -6% 3-yr CAGR (staffing contraction in weak labor market), but EPS jumped +155% YoY (cost cuts and mix shift). Net margin of 1.3% is very thin (typical for staffing), gross margin of 19%, ROIC of 9.4%. Against Adecco (ADEN.SW, ~7-8x normalized FCF) and ManpowerGroup (MAN, ~8-10x), Randstad screens cheap on p_fcf. Price at 26% of 52-wk range, below SMA200 — momentum is negative.
RAND.AS.rationale: True peers are global and European staffing multinationals: Adecco (ADEN.SW), ManpowerGroup (MAN), Hays (HAYS.L), PageGroup (PAGE.L), and Kelly Services (KELF). Randstad is the #2 global staffing firm by revenue; at 5.6x p_fcf it screens cheap versus Adecco and ManpowerGroup, with the discount driven by European labor-market softness rather than structural deterioration — a cyclical discount, not a permanent impairment.

RHI.relative_comps: Robert Half (professional staffing — accounting, finance, technology temps) trades at p_fcf of 11.8x with DCF MoS of 59% and owner-earnings MoS of 43% — legitimate undervaluation for a premium staffing brand. Revenue fell -7.2% YoY and -9% 3-yr CAGR (white-collar hiring freeze), EPS fell -45%, net margin compressed to 2.5% (cyclical trough) but ROIC of 21% — exceptionally high — reflects the asset-light model in normal conditions. Gross margin of 37% is the highest among listed staffing peers. Against ASGN (~12-14x FCF) and Kforce (KFRC, ~10-12x), RHI screens slightly cheap to in-line. Price at 40% of 52-wk range, below SMA200 — cyclical headwinds persist.
RHI.rationale: True peers are white-collar professional and technology staffing firms: ASGN (formerly On Assignment), Kforce (KFRC), Hays (HAYS.L for European comparison), and ManpowerGroup's professional segment. RHI's Protiviti consulting arm differentiates it structurally from pure-play staffers; at 12x p_fcf during an earnings trough with 21% normal-state ROIC, it screens in-line to slightly cheap — the trough masks the quality.

ROCK.relative_comps: Gibraltar Industries (steel products for construction — ventilation, roofing, solar racking) trades at p_fcf of 9.2x with DCF MoS of 55% and EPV approximately in-line; graham MoS of 50% adds support. Revenue fell -13% YoY and -7% 3-yr CAGR reflecting residential construction softness; EPS -27%, net margin of 8.6%, ROIC of 9.4%. Against Eagle Materials (EXP, ~12-15x FCF), USG (now Knauf private), and Vulcan Materials (VMC, ~25x FCF), ROCK is clearly cheaper on FCF — but it is a much lower quality/moat business than VMC/MLM aggregates. The solar-racking exposure (Postal Platform segment) is a hidden growth asset. Price is only 8% from 52-wk low.
ROCK.rationale: True peers are steel and building-products manufacturers serving residential and commercial construction: Eagle Materials (EXP), Summit Materials (SUM), US Concrete (private), and specialty racking/framing players. ROCK's solar-racking growth optionality is underappreciated by the market; 9x p_fcf with 55% DCF MoS at a cyclical construction low represents genuine cheap-vs-peers pricing.

SKYW.relative_comps: SkyWest (regional airline operating under United, Delta, Alaska, American brands via capacity-purchase agreements) trades at p_fcf of 12.2x with graham_revised MoS of 76% and IV15 MoS of 74% — strong value signals. Revenue grew +15% YoY and 10.5% 3-yr CAGR as pilot shortages at regionals ease; EPS +33%, gross margin of 62% (high — CPA structure means revenue is mostly fee-per-block-hour with fuel pass-through), net margin of 10.6%, ROIC of 4% (asset-heavy with owned aircraft). Versus Mesa Air (MESA, distressed, ~2-3x FCF), and private peers, SKYW is the only quality pure-play publicly listed US regional carrier. Altman-Z of 1.78 reflects leverage, normal for airlines. Price at 14% of 52-wk range — poor momentum despite good fundamentals.
SKYW.rationale: True peers are US regional airline operators under capacity-purchase agreements: Mesa Air (MESA), Air Wisconsin, GoJet Airlines, and the regional subsidiaries of American (Envoy), United (CommutAir), and Delta (Endeavor). SKYW is the only at-scale, investment-grade-adjacent public regional carrier; 12x p_fcf with 75%+ MoS on two methods at a 52-wk low represents genuine cheap-vs-peers, with the caveat that airline leverage keeps Altman-Z borderline.

TEP.PA.relative_comps: TEP.PA (Teleperformance) is the global CX/BPO market leader, yet at p_fcf ~2.8x and DCF MoS of 80% it screens as dramatically cheap relative to peers: Concentrix trades at ~8-10x FCF and TaskUs near 12x, while TTEC and Atento are structurally weaker businesses. The discount is partly justified by AI displacement fears (chat/voice automation risk to offshore headcount) and a governance controversy around its subsidiary Majorel, but the scale advantage, long-term government contracts, and 3-year revenue CAGR of +7.8% suggest the market is pricing permanent disruption that has not materialised in numbers — the gap looks like a genuine mispricing rather than a fully-justified structural discount. Net margin of 4.9% and ROIC of 7.9% are below Concentrix's recent highs but consistent with a normalised post-integration phase; the stock at 27% of 52-week range versus still-profitable peers confirms deep value.
TEP.PA.rationale: Teleperformance's true peer set is the global CX outsourcing / BPO oligopoly — Concentrix, TTEC, TaskUs, TDCX, Atento, Conduent — businesses competing for the same multinational client budgets on voice, chat, and back-office work. Majorel (now merged) and Synnex (Concentrix) are the closest scale comps; US-listed staffing or generic business-services names are too different in economics and end-market.

TNET.relative_comps: TriNet operates a PEO (Professional Employer Organisation) model — co-employing SMB workforces and bundling HR, benefits, and payroll — making its true peers PAYC, PAYX (for SMB payroll/HCM), and Insperity (NSP) for direct PEO comp. At p_fcf ~7.3x and DCF MoS 68% TNET is the cheapest in the group: Insperity trades ~18x FCF, Paychex ~25x, and ADP ~28x. TNET's discount is partly justified by its PEO model's higher medical claims volatility and recent revenue softness (-0.9% YoY) from SMB hiring deceleration, but ROIC of 21.4% is best-in-class among PEOs and the owner-earnings MoS of 64% implies genuine undervaluation — the gap to Insperity alone suggests 2-3 turns of multiple compression versus a higher-quality business with comparable capital efficiency.
TNET.rationale: TNET's defining business characteristic is the PEO co-employment structure, which means it competes for SMB HR/benefits outsourcing dollars against Insperity, ADP TotalSource, Paychex PEO, and smaller regional PEOs — not generic staffing firms like Adecco or Manpower. RAND.AS and RHI in the universe are temporary-staffing businesses with structurally lower margins and different capital dynamics, making them loose comps at best.

VRT.relative_comps: Vertiv is the pure-play data-centre power and thermal management leader — UPS systems, precision cooling, PDUs — riding the hyperscaler and AI build-out wave. At p_fcf ~62x it is rich versus traditional electrical equipment peers: Eaton trades ~30x FCF, Legrand ~22x, and nVent ~20x. The premium is largely justified: VRT's revenue CAGR of 21.6% and EPS growth of 166% YoY dwarf Eaton (+mid-single-digits) and Schneider (+high-single-digits), and data-centre exposure commands a secular premium. However, at 71% of the 52-week range after a massive re-rating, and with all valuation MoS methods returning -1.0, the stock prices in near-perfection; any margin miss or hyperscaler capex pause would expose the valuation gap versus lower-multiple diversified peers with similar quality but less hypergrowth.
VRT.rationale: Vertiv competes in mission-critical data-centre infrastructure — specifically power conversion, UPS, and precision cooling — alongside Eaton's data-centre segment, Schneider Electric's EcoStruxure line, Legrand's data infrastructure division, nVent, and ABB's electrification segment. Generic industrials or broad electrical companies are too diversified; the relevant peer set is defined by data-centre revenue concentration and thermal/power product mix.

WKL.AS.relative_comps: Wolters Kluwer is a professional information and software platform (legal, tax, compliance, clinical) with 73.5% gross margins and 21.4% net margins — fundamentally a high-quality recurring-revenue compounder. At p_fcf ~10x and DCF MoS of 46%, it is unambiguously cheap relative to direct peers: RELX trades ~25x FCF, Verisk ~30x, MSCI ~40x, and Gartner (IT in universe at p_fcf 10x but with higher ROIC of 21%) is the closest in-universe comp at a similar multiple though with superior margin trajectory. The 52-week proximity of just 7.5% flags that the stock has de-rated sharply, likely on FX headwinds (EUR reporting, USD revenues) and modest 3.5% organic growth — but these are cyclical, not structural, and the high ROIC of 16.9% and EPS growth of 24.5% confirm that earnings quality remains intact; the discount to RELX and Verisk is not justified by business quality and represents a real mispricing.
WKL.AS.rationale: Wolters Kluwer's correct peer set is the professional information and workflow software oligopoly — RELX, Verisk, Thomson Reuters, MSCI, FactSet — businesses that sell mission-critical data and software to regulated professions (legal, tax, compliance, financial). Gartner (IT) in the universe is the closest in-screen comp on model but serves a different end-market (IT research advisory). Generic business-services or staffing firms are not comparables given WKL's software-like gross margins and subscription revenue model.

WLDN.relative_comps: Willdan Group is a niche engineering and consulting firm serving US municipalities and utilities on clean energy, grid modernisation, and infrastructure — a capital-light professional services model. At p_fcf ~20.7x and a DCF MoS of -0.90 (deeply negative), WLDN is priced well above its intrinsic value on traditional methods; however, revenue CAGR of 16.7% and EPS nearly doubling (+121% YoY) put it in growth-company territory. Tetra Tech (TTEK) — its most direct US peer on government-funded environmental and energy services — trades ~22-25x FCF with better margins (gross ~18%) and larger scale, so WLDN is roughly in-line to slightly cheap on a growth-adjusted basis. NV5 trades at comparable multiples with superior ROIC; Arcadis in Europe is cheaper (~14x) but slower growth. WLDN's 4.1% ROIC is the key risk — it suggests the business has not yet converted its topline surge into durable returns on capital.
WLDN.rationale: Willdan's true peer set is the US government-and-utility-focused environmental and energy engineering consultancy sector — Tetra Tech, NV5, ICF International, WSP's US government segment, and Arcadis — businesses winning federal and state contracts on clean energy transition and infrastructure. Generic large-cap engineers (Jacobs, AECOM) are too diversified and capital-heavy; the relevant comps are asset-light technical consultancies with significant public-sector revenue concentration.

ACMR.relative_comps: ACMR (ACM Research) is a wet-cleaning and single-wafer processing equipment maker competing with Lam Research, KLA, Applied Materials, and niche players like Onto Innovation and Camtek in advanced node inspection/process. Its p_fcf is unmeasurable (FCF-negative at this stage), gross margin of 44% and ROIC of 6% are thin vs. KLAC/LRCX at 55-70%+ gross and 25-40% ROIC; revenue CAGR of 32% (3yr) is best-in-class but that is a China-captive growth story under export-restriction risk. Price sits at 75% of 52-wk high and above SMA200, reflecting speculative premium on China growth with no valuation anchor — Graham MoS is barely negative, suggesting market is not deeply cheap either.
ACMR.rationale: True peers are semiconductor process equipment makers (KLAC, LRCX, AMAT, ASML, BESI); ACMR trades on speculative China-growth with structurally inferior margins/ROIC and no FCF valuation support.

AMD.relative_comps: AMD is a fabless processor/GPU company competing directly with NVIDIA in AI accelerators and Intel in x86 CPUs. At p_fcf of 113x vs NVDA at 52x and AVGO at 70x, AMD trades richer on FCF despite generating only 12.5% net margins vs NVDA's 56% and AVGO's 36%. Revenue grew 34% YoY with a 3yr CAGR of 14%, solid but behind NVDA's 65% YoY, yet AMD's gross margin of 50% lags NVDA's 71%. The 81% 52-wk proximity suggests strong momentum, but all MoS methods reject value — the premium over NVDA on a FCF basis is difficult to justify given AMD's inferior margin/ROIC profile (9.5% vs NVDA's 45%).
AMD.rationale: Fabless semiconductor peers (NVDA, AVGO, QCOM, MRVL) all have better margins, ROIC, or cheaper FCF multiples than AMD; AMD's AI-GPU market share hopes are already priced in relative to the peer set.

ANET.relative_comps: Arista Networks is a hyperscale/cloud data-center networking pure-play competing with Cisco's Catalyst/Nexus lines and Juniper Networks. ANET's p_fcf of 46x is premium but its 39% net margin, 22% ROIC, and 27% revenue CAGR are genuinely best-in-class — Cisco trades at much lower multiples but with low-single-digit growth and 20% net margins. The premium is largely justified by ANET's cloudification/AI-infrastructure tailwind and sticky customer base; at 73% of 52-wk high and above SMA200, momentum confirms the quality story. All Speculair MoS methods reject it as cheap, consistent with a quality compounder priced for excellence.
ANET.rationale: Closest peers are Cisco and Juniper; ANET's dramatically superior growth and margins justify a structural premium, but the absolute FCF multiple leaves limited margin of safety versus those peers.

ARM.relative_comps: ARM Holdings is the dominant IP licensor for CPU/NPU architectures — royalties and licenses with near-monopoly economics. At p_fcf of 374x, it is the richest-valued name in the group and in global semis; gross margin of 92% and net margin of 18% reflect its asset-light IP model, but ROIC of 8% is surprisingly modest given the capital-light structure (heavy goodwill from SoftBank acquisition). Revenue CAGR of 22% is respectable but does not justify a 374x FCF multiple when NVDA (the other monopolist) trades at 52x with 45% ROIC and 56% net margin — ARM's valuation incorporates a very long runway of AI/IoT royalty ramp that must materialise without misstep.
ARM.rationale: ARM's only true peers are other near-monopoly IP/platform semiconductor businesses; on virtually every normalised value metric it trades at a 3-6x premium to even NVDA, implying embedded perfection with no margin of safety.

ARW.relative_comps: Arrow Electronics is a global technology distributor (electronics components + IT products) competing directly with Avnet (AVT) and TD SYNNEX (SNX). ARW's p_fcf is zero/negative (FCF-negative in period), gross margin of 11% and net margin of 1.9% are typical thin-margin distribution economics; revenue grew 10% YoY but 3yr CAGR is -6% reflecting component cycle. In-universe INGM (Ingram Micro) is the most comparable: INGM p_fcf 8.8x vs ARW near-zero, both with ~6-10% gross margins. ARW is above SMA200 at 89% of 52-wk high suggesting market expects a cycle turn, but with no FCF anchor the 'cheapness' is illusory — it depends on normalised earnings through a component cycle trough.
ARW.rationale: Distribution peers (AVT, SNX, INGM) all have similar thin-margin economics; ARW is at cycle trough on FCF but its asset base and through-cycle earnings power suggest it trades cheap relative to normalised mid-cycle earnings multiple vs SNX/AVT.

ASML.relative_comps: ASML is the monopoly supplier of EUV lithography equipment — there is no true direct substitute. At p_fcf of 56x, its 29% net margin and 31% ROIC are outstanding for capital equipment; revenue CAGR of 16% reflects the pace of advanced-node capex. Versus KLAC (p_fcf ~40x, 30% net margins) and Tokyo Electron (~25x, 20% margins), ASML trades at a justified monopoly premium — the EUV franchise is unassailable through 2030+. At 90% of 52-wk high and above SMA200, momentum is constructive; all MoS methods reject pure deep value but the MoS spread is the tightest negative of the group, consistent with 'expensive but defensible'.
ASML.rationale: ASML's monopoly in EUV lithography justifies a premium vs KLAC/LRCX/Tokyo Electron; the current FCF multiple is elevated but in line with its franchise value and peers' own elevated multiples in a capex supercycle.

AVGO.relative_comps: Broadcom is a diversified semiconductor and infrastructure software company (post-VMware) competing with Qualcomm in wireless/connectivity, Marvell in cloud ASICs, and TI in analog. At p_fcf of 70x, its 36% net margin and 15% ROIC (depressed by VMware leverage) are strong, with 24% revenue CAGR and 3yr CAGR at 24%. Versus QCOM (p_fcf ~15x, 26% net margin) or Marvell (~50x but lower margins), AVGO is expensive but its custom AI ASIC franchise and VMware cross-sell create a defensible moat. Price at 57% of 52-wk high and above SMA200 suggests modest recent weakness — the AI ASIC tailwind is a genuine differentiator from QCOM/TI peers.
AVGO.rationale: AVGO's true peers span custom ASICs (Marvell), connectivity (Qualcomm), and diversified semis (TI, INTC); its FCF multiple is elevated but justified by superior growth visibility and unique AI custom silicon exposure vs the peer set.

CRDO.relative_comps: Credo Technology is a high-speed connectivity chip maker (SerDes, optical DSPs for 800G/1.6T Ethernet) competing with Marvell's electro-optics division, MACOM (MTSI), and Semtech. CRDO's p_fcf of 1342x is extraordinary — 206% revenue growth YoY and 94% 3yr CAGR are genuine hypergrowth but produce 35% net margins and only 1.6% ROIC (early-stage). Versus MTSI (p_fcf 133x, 33% revenue growth, -6% net margin) or Semtech (p_fcf 84x), CRDO trades at a significant premium even within the high-speed connectivity niche. At 78% of 52-wk high and above SMA200, momentum supports but the valuation embeds years of flawless execution.
CRDO.rationale: Closest peers are high-speed connectivity/DSP chip makers (MTSI, SMTC, MRVL electro-optics, MXL); even at hypergrowth rates CRDO's ~10x premium FCF multiple to peers like MTSI is hard to justify on near-term fundamentals alone.

CTSH.relative_comps: Cognizant is a large-cap IT services/outsourcing firm directly competing with Infosys, Wipro, TCS, and HCL Technologies in application management, BPO, and digital transformation. At p_fcf of 9.9x, it is the cheapest name in the tech group by a wide margin; 34% gross margins and 11% net margin lag TCS/Infosys (40-50% gross, 20%+ net) but CTSH trades at roughly half the forward multiple of Indian IT peers partly due to lower growth (7% rev YoY, 3% 3yr CAGR) and US-centric exposure. ROIC of 14% is solid, and CTSH sits at 19% of 52-wk high — deeply discounted. The discount to Indian IT peers is partly deserved (slower growth, higher wage inflation exposure) but p_fcf of ~10x for a cash-generative IT services business with stable margins is historically cheap.
CTSH.rationale: True IT services peers (INFY, WIT, TCS, EPAM) trade at materially higher multiples on similar or only modestly better growth; CTSH's discount is partly justified by lower growth but appears excessive at sub-10x FCF for a cash-rich, ROIC-positive business.

DAVE.relative_comps: Dave Inc. is a neobank/cash-advance fintech competing with Chime, MoneyLion, Brigit, and Earnin in the underbanked consumer segment. At p_fcf of 12.9x, it appears cheap in isolation, but SOFI trades at higher multiples on stronger revenue diversification; 60% revenue growth and 35% net margin are impressive for the category. DAVE's ROIC is negative (-2.8%) and Altman-Z of 9.9 signals financial robustness. The p_fcf looks cheap vs neobank peers but DAVE's TAM is more limited (payday-advance niche) versus SoFi's broader ambitions. Price at 75% of 52-wk high suggests recent strength; the FCF yield at 12.9x is genuinely attractive if growth sustains.
DAVE.rationale: Neobank/fintech peers (SOFI, MoneyLion, AFRM) generally trade at higher multiples; DAVE's combination of 60% growth, 35% net margin, and sub-13x FCF is anomalously cheap if the growth trajectory holds.

peers: Euronet Worldwide is its own category: competitors include Western Union (WU), MoneyGram (MGI)
EEFT.relative_comps: Euronet Worldwide (EEFT) operates ATMs, epay digital media top-ups, and money transfer (competing with WU, MoneyGram in cross-border payments). At p_fcf of 7.4x, EEFT is the cheapest non-distributor in the tech group; 41% gross margins and 7.3% net margin are lower than payments peers (FISV 59% gross, 16% net) due to ATM capex intensity. Revenue growth is modest (6% YoY, 8% 3yr CAGR) and ROIC of 8% is below FISV's 6% (but FISV is bloated by VMware-like M&A). At only 6% of 52-wk high, EEFT is in deep price distress — near 52-wk lows — suggesting the market is pricing in secular ATM decline concerns. The discount looks excessive versus payment comps if EEFT's epay/money transfer segments continue to grow.
EEFT.rationale: Cross-border payments and payment processing peers (WU, FISV, WEX, FOUR) all trade at meaningfully higher FCF multiples; EEFT's 7x FCF with 8% ROIC and positive MoS spread looks genuinely cheap, though ATM secular risk is a real overhang.

FICO.relative_comps: FICO is a credit-scoring and decision analytics monopoly (the FICO score) competing with credit bureaus (Equifax, TransUnion, Experian) and analytics platforms (MSCI, Verisk). At p_fcf of 36x, it is cheaper than Verisk (~45x) or MSCI (~55x) but has far better ROIC (41%) and net margin (33%) than any pure bureau. Revenue growing 16% YoY and 13% 3yr CAGR reflects the mortgage score price increase cycle. FICO sits at 24% of 52-wk high — moderate weakness. All MoS methods largely reject it as cheap, but relative to MSCI/Verisk at similar or higher multiples with lower ROIC, FICO looks in-line to modestly cheap given its superior returns profile.
FICO.rationale: Data/analytics monopoly peers (MSCI, Verisk, credit bureaus) trade at equivalent or higher FCF multiples with lower ROIC; FICO's 41% ROIC and 33% margins justify a premium that isn't fully reflected at 36x FCF, making it mildly cheap versus the peer set.

peers: FIS (formerly Fiserv competitor, now merged landscape)
FISV.relative_comps: Fiserv is a merchant acquiring and banking technology platform competing with Global Payments, FIS, PayPal, and Jack Henry. At p_fcf of 6.9x, FISV is by far the cheapest large-cap payments processor; 59% gross margins and 16% net margin are solid, ROIC of 6.4% reflects M&A-inflated asset base (First Data acquisition). Revenue grew only 4% YoY and 6% 3yr CAGR — well below GPN/PYPL peers — but the stock is at effectively a 52-wk low (1% proximity). The deep discount vs Mastercard/Visa (40-60x) and even GPN (~15x) is partly deserved on growth, but FISV's FCF yield at 6.9x for a structurally profitable payment platform is historically cheap and appears to be genuine mispricing driven by rate-sensitivity fears.
FISV.rationale: Payment processing peers (GPN, JKHY, PYPL, FIS) trade at 2-8x FISV's FCF multiple on similar or lower quality; FISV at sub-7x FCF for a 60% gross margin business with stable bank-tech relationships represents a clear relative cheapness.

INGM.relative_comps: Ingram Micro is a direct competitor to Arrow Electronics (ARW) in technology distribution (IT products, cloud, lifecycle services). At p_fcf of 8.8x and 7% gross margin, INGM is thin-margin but shows 10% revenue growth and 18% EPS YoY. ROIC of 12.7% is actually stronger than ARW's (ROIC not computable for ARW on FCF basis). INGM at 84% of 52-wk high vs ARW at 89% both reflect cycle recovery expectations. In-universe ARW is the direct comp: both are in the same business with nearly identical gross margin profiles; INGM's p_fcf of 8.8x vs ARW near-zero makes INGM look slightly richer on that metric, but INGM's higher ROIC and positive FCF makes it the more defensible of the two.
INGM.rationale: Direct peers are ARW, AVT, SNX — all global tech distributors; INGM's valuation at ~9x FCF with 13% ROIC is in line with through-cycle distribution economics, neither clearly cheap nor rich versus ARW/AVT.

MPWR.relative_comps: Monolithic Power Systems is a high-efficiency analog/mixed-signal IC maker for AI servers, EVs, and consumer electronics, competing with TI, Analog Devices, and Renesas. At p_fcf of 107x, it is the richest analog semi in the group; 55% gross margin and 22% net margin are excellent, ROIC of 18% is strong. Revenue grew 26% YoY vs TI's flat and ADI's -12%, reflecting AI power-delivery exposure. The stock at 78% of 52-wk high and above SMA200 supports the quality narrative, but 107x FCF vs TXN at 20x and ADI at 30x requires MPWR to sustain market-share gains for years. The premium vs peers is real but partially justified by AI/EV differentiation.
MPWR.rationale: Analog semi peers (TXN, ADI, MCHP, Renesas) trade at 15-35x FCF with comparable or slightly lower margins; MPWR's 107x reflects an AI-power premium that is well above what fundamentals at this growth rate can sustain vs peers.

MTSI.relative_comps: MACOM Technology Solutions is a compound semiconductor/RF and optical components maker competing with Qorvo, Skyworks, Marvell's optical group, and II-VI/Coherent in defense, telecom, and data center. At p_fcf of 133x and -5.6% net margin, MACOM is not yet profitable at the net level despite 55% gross margins and 33% revenue growth (recovering from telecom trough). ROIC of 6.6% is modest. Versus Qorvo (p_fcf ~20x) or Skyworks (~13x), MTSI looks very expensive on near-term metrics, but its data-center optical ramp justifies a higher growth premium than commodity RF peers. The stock at 76% of 52-wk high is recovering.
MTSI.rationale: Compound semiconductor and RF peers (QRVO, SWKS, VIAV) trade at 10-25x FCF with positive net margins; MTSI's 133x on -5.6% net margin is rich even accounting for its optical/data-center ramp story.

NEXI.MI.relative_comps: Nexi is the leading Italian payment processor (POS, e-commerce, digital banking services) competing with Worldline across Europe and indirectly with Adyen and PayPal. At p_fcf of 4.8x, NEXI looks extremely cheap; 58% gross margin is solid but -54% net margin reflects heavy amortisation of M&A intangibles (Nets, SIA acquisitions). ROIC of 2.8% is thin, Altman-Z of 0.63 is near-distress territory. Worldline (WLN.PA) trades at similar crisis-level discounts given European payments margin compression. NEXI's p_fcf cheapness is real but the debt load from serial M&A creates genuine financial risk — it is cheap for a reason, not a clean mispricing.
NEXI.MI.rationale: European payment peers (Worldline, Adyen) and global comps (FISV, GPN) highlight NEXI's cheap FCF multiple, but the cheapness reflects M&A-driven leverage and negative net margin; it is cheap-with-risk rather than a pure mispricing.

NTES.relative_comps: NetEase is a Chinese online gaming, music, and education company competing primarily with Tencent Games domestically and internationally against EA/Activision. At p_fcf of 11.4x, NTES is cheap for a 64% gross margin, 30% net margin business with 14% ROIC; revenue grew 7% YoY and 5% 3yr CAGR, slower than global gaming peers but NTES's profitability metrics are extraordinary vs Western peers. Tencent (unlisted directly but comparable) trades at higher multiples. At 25% of 52-wk high and below recent highs, the China discount is embedded — regulatory risk, geopolitical friction, and VIE structure. Even adjusting for China risk, NTES at 11x FCF for 30% net margins is objectively cheap vs EA (~25x), RBLX (loss-making), or SE.
NTES.rationale: Global gaming/digital entertainment peers (EA, Activision/MSFT, Tencent, Sea) trade at 20-50x multiples with lower profitability; NTES at 11x FCF with 30% net margin and 14% ROIC is deeply cheap on fundamentals, with the China discount offering a potential value opportunity if geopolitical risk normalises.

NVDA.relative_comps: NVIDIA dominates AI accelerator GPUs (H100/B100/B200 series) with no direct substitute in training-scale compute. At p_fcf of 52x, it is significantly cheaper than ARM (374x) and comparable to ASML (56x) — both monopolies — while delivering 71% gross margin, 56% net margin, and 45% ROIC that no competitor matches. Revenue grew 65% YoY with 100% 3yr CAGR; AMD at 113x with 50% gross/12.5% net is more expensive and far less profitable. NVDA at 68% of 52-wk high has corrected from peak but remains above SMA200; relative to AMD, AVGO, and ASML it is the highest-quality name at a reasonable (not cheap) premium. The p_fcf of 52x for a 45%-ROIC monopoly is not classically cheap but is defensible.
NVDA.rationale: NVDA's true peers are AI compute monopolists and high-margin semis (AMD, AVGO, ASML, ARM); on every quality metric (ROIC, gross margin, net margin) NVDA leads the group, and its 52x FCF is a lower multiple than ARM and AMD despite being a superior business — making it in-line to slightly cheap within the high-quality semiconductor peer set.

RMBS.relative_comps: Rambus provides memory interface chips and security IP licensing to semiconductor companies and hyperscalers, competing with patent licensing pure-plays (InterDigital, Xperi) and memory interface chip makers. At p_fcf of 48x, its 76% gross margin and 33% net margin are excellent for a semi-IP hybrid; ROIC of 9.3% is moderate. Revenue grew 27% YoY, 16% 3yr CAGR, driven by HBM/CXL interface demand from AI infrastructure. IDCC (InterDigital) trades at ~25x with similar licensing economics but telecom exposure; LSCC (Lattice) at ~30x with 65% gross. RMBS at 48x is richer than licensing peers but justified by the AI memory interface tailwind. Price at 76% of 52-wk high with positive momentum (above SMA200) supports the thesis.
RMBS.rationale: Memory IP and semiconductor licensing peers (IDCC, LSCC, XPER) trade at 20-35x FCF with comparable margin profiles; RMBS's 48x reflects an AI/HBM memory interface premium that is elevated but partially justified by the structural demand shift.

SMTC.relative_comps: SMTC trades at P/FCF ~84x with zero positive MoS across all valuation methods, placing it firmly at the rich end of the analog/mixed-signal semi peer group — MPWR commands a similar 107x but earns 22% net margins and 18% ROIC vs SMTC's -3.8% net loss and 6.3% ROIC; MCHP and ON (not in universe) trade at single-digit to mid-teens P/E with positive net income, making SMTC's premium look entirely unjustified on current earnings. Revenue growth of +15.5% YoY and decent 51.6% gross margins signal an improving LoRa IoT ramp, and the stock sits near its 52-week high (proximity 0.84), meaning momentum is strong — but the valuation richness reflects speculative pricing on a future recovery that has not yet converted to earnings, not a fundamental discount.
SMTC.rationale: True peers are analog/mixed-signal and IoT connectivity chip designers sharing similar fab-light economics, gross margin profiles, and end-market exposure (LoRa IoT, optical data center, protection ICs): Silicon Labs (SLAB), Microchip Technology (MCHP), Monolithic Power (MPWR), MACOM Technology (MTSI), ON Semiconductor (ON), Credo Technology (CRDO), Rambus (RMBS). These are the companies competing for the same design wins in industrial IoT, protection devices, and high-speed interconnect.

SWON.SW.relative_comps: SWON.SW (SoftwareOne) trades at P/FCF ~5.7x with an EPV MoS barely above zero — cheap on FCF multiples relative to IT services and software licensing peers, but the near-zero net margin (0.07%) reveals a business under severe margin compression as it pivots from pure licensing to managed cloud services; in-universe CDW (ARW, P/FCF ~0, also distributor-adjacent) and CTSH (P/FCF ~10x, 10.6% net margin, 13.8% ROIC) both look better-quality. Revenue growth of +22.5% YoY is strong but is partly inorganic, and the altman_z of 0.65 flags real balance-sheet fragility, suggesting the apparent cheapness is value-trap territory rather than genuine mispricing.
SWON.SW.rationale: SoftwareOne is an IT software asset management and cloud solutions reseller/advisory firm; true peers are other global software licensing aggregators and cloud managed-service resellers: Crayon Group (CRAYON.OL), WEX (WEX) for software spend management, CDW (CDW), Computacenter (CCC.L), Bechtle (BC8.DE), and Insight Direct (NSIT). These firms share the same economics — razor-thin product margins boosted by value-added services attach — and compete directly for multi-cloud advisory mandates.

UPBD.relative_comps: UPBD (Upbound, formerly Rent-A-Center) is the standout cheap name in this group: P/FCF of ~4.5x, owner_earnings MoS of 92%, iv15 MoS of 80%, and graham_revised MoS of 62% — all strongly positive and consistent, suggesting deep value relative to intrinsic worth. However, net margin of only 1.6% and revenue growth of +8.7% reveal the core rent-to-own business (Rent-A-Center + Acima virtual lease-to-own) is fundamentally low-quality relative to fintech software peers like OMF (OneMain) or CACC — the apparent cheapness is partially justified by the subprime consumer credit risk embedded in the book; the stock is in the bottom quintile of its 52-week range (proximity 0.20), suggesting the market already discounts this risk premium.
UPBD.rationale: UPBD's true peer set is rent-to-own / lease-to-own and subprime consumer credit companies sharing nearly identical customer demographics, product economics, and regulatory exposure: Aaron's Holdings (AAN), the legacy Rent-A-Center legacy comps, OneMain Financial (OMF), Credit Acceptance Corp (CACC), EZCorp (EZPW), and World Acceptance (WRLD). These are not software businesses despite UPBD's SIC classification; they compete for the same underbanked consumer with similar underwriting models and charge-off dynamics.

VNT.relative_comps: VNT (Vontier) trades at P/FCF ~9.4x with DCF MoS of 41% and owner_earnings MoS of 59% — moderately cheap on most valuation metrics relative to industrial tech peers, with 47% gross margins and 13.2% net margins consistent with a Fortive (FTV) spin-off retaining high-quality measurement and fuel-dispensing technology assets. Revenue growth of only +3.2% YoY and near-zero 3Y CAGR (-1.2%) drag on the growth premium; with proximity_52wk at 0.043 the stock is essentially at its 52-week low, suggesting the market is pricing in secular headwind risk from EV displacement of fuel dispensers (Gilbarco Veeder-Root segment), making the cheapness partially — but perhaps not fully — justified given the resilient EV charger and fleet management optionality.
VNT.rationale: Vontier's true peers are precision industrial technology and measurement companies that also serve fuel retail, EV infrastructure, and fleet management: Trimble (TRMB), Itron (ITRI), Fortive (FTV, the parent), Roper Technologies (ROP), and for the fuel dispensing / EV charge management sub-segment: Nuvve (NVVE), Blink Charging (BLNK), and Gilbarco competitors PDI Technologies. These firms share similar EBITDA margin profiles, recurring software attach strategies, and exposure to the energy transition tailwind or headwind.

VRSN.relative_comps: VRSN is a regulatory-monopoly business (the sole .com and .net registry operator under ICANN contracts) with no true economic competitor: 88% gross margins, 50% net margins, and a P/FCF of ~25.6x, trading near its 52-week high (proximity 0.83) — all MoS methods are -1.0 or near zero because no DCF or intrinsic-value model can capture a perpetual quasi-utility license at a 6% annual price increase with near-zero capex. Relative to the closest listed analogues (GoDaddy GDDY trades at ~20x EV/EBITDA with much lower margins and high reinvestment needs, Donuts/private, Afternic/private), VRSN looks rich on absolute P/FCF but the richness is structural and likely permanent — investors pay for the near-zero competitive risk and 100% FCF conversion, not growth (revenue CAGR 5.2% 3Y).
VRSN.rationale: VeriSign has no direct listed peer with equivalent monopoly characteristics; the closest comparables are internet registry and domain infrastructure operators: GoDaddy (GDDY) as the dominant registrar (not registry), Afilias (private), identity trust providers like DigiCert (private), and for the monopoly-franchise valuation framework, regulated utilities and exchange operators (CME, ICE) that share the pricing-power-without-competition dynamic. VRSN is in a class of its own — any 'comp' analysis must acknowledge the premium is the franchise, not a mispricing.

ACA.PA.relative_comps: Crédit Agricole trades at p/FCF ~2.6x and carries strong EPV/Graham MoS spreads (0.60/0.79), placing it at the cheap end of the European universal-bank peer set where BNP and SocGen trade at 6-9x normalised earnings. Revenue growth of +7% and a 35% 3-year CAGR are clearly above peer median, but a 6.3% net margin and near-zero ROIC (~0.3%) reflect the capital-intensive, thin-spread nature of retail banking. Price sits 32% above its 52-week low and above sma200, suggesting some momentum recovery, yet on MoS metrics it screens deeply discounted even against already-cheap European banks. The discount is only partially justified by elevated group complexity (insurance, CIB, agri-banking) and ongoing French macro risk; on a pure P/B or earnings-yield basis there is genuine residual cheapness.
ACA.PA.rationale: True peers are large European universal banks (BNP, SocGen, Deutsche, UniCredit, BBVA, ING, Intesa); all face similar NIM/capital cycles. ACA screens cheap on every relative-value method in the universe file vs. that cohort.

ALMB.CO.relative_comps: Alm. Brand (Danish P&C/life insurer) shows a distorted p/FCF of 149x (likely reflects acquisition-year amortisation drag after the Codan integration), but eps grew +78% as the combined entity is beginning to realise cost synergies. All MoS methods return -1 except apex, signalling the screen cannot value it conventionally. Against Nordic P&C peers—Tryg, Gjensidige, Sampo P&C—which trade at 10-18x earnings with 15-20% ROE, ALMB is in-line to slightly cheap on a forward normalised basis once integration costs roll off, but the near-52-week-low price (19% above trough) and negative revenue trajectory (-5.4%) signal execution risk that justifies the discount. The gap is partially justified by integration uncertainty, not fundamental business weakness.
ALMB.CO.rationale: Nordic P&C insurers (Tryg, Gjensidige, Sampo) are the true benchmarks; ALMB's optical cheapness is clouded by Codan integration noise—in-line on a through-cycle basis.

AXS.relative_comps: Axis Capital (specialty P&C/reinsurance hybrid) has p/FCF reported as 0 (negative operating FCF year), yet DCF/EPV/Graham MoS are solidly positive (0.63/0.39/0.61), pointing to normalised value well above price. Revenue grew +9% and the 3-year CAGR is +7.4%, in line with the hard-market tailwinds enjoyed by RenaissanceRe, Everest, Markel, and WR Berkley. Net margin of 15.4% and a combined ratio trending toward the low-90s compare favourably with peers. Price is close to sma200 and near mid-range of the 52-week band (48%). The multi-method MoS spread signals AXSS is cheap relative to a peer group that has rerated sharply on hard-cycle expectations; the discount is not justified by quality and represents a real mispricing opportunity.
AXS.rationale: Specialty P&C and cat reinsurance peers (RNR, EG, MKL, WRB, ACGL, RE) are the right comparables; AXS's multi-method MoS spread of 0.40-0.63 stands out as genuinely cheap in a group that has rerated to 1.3-1.6x book.

BGN.MI.relative_comps: Banca Generali (Italian private bank/wealth manager) at p/FCF ~11.8x is priced between pure private-banking multiples (Julius Baer ~15x, Mediolanum ~12x) and retail banking (ISP/UCG ~5-8x), which is appropriate for its hybrid model. Revenue surged +27% and 3-year CAGR is +29%, materially above peers, and net margin of 27% reflects the asset-light, fee-heavy wealth management model. Graham MoS of 0.77 and strong owner-earnings MoS signal the screen finds it cheap; however proximity_52wk of 0.73 (near the top of its range) and price above sma200 indicate momentum is strong. The valuation is in-line to mildly cheap relative to Italian private-banking peers; the premium to retail banks is fully justified by margin and growth superiority.
BGN.MI.rationale: Italian/European private banks and wealth managers (Mediolanum, FinecoBank, Julius Baer, Banca Mediolanum) are the right peer set; BGN's blend of banking and wealth management sits at fair value in that group, with growth justifying its modest premium to pure retail banks.

ESNT.relative_comps: Essent Group (US private mortgage insurance) at p/FCF ~6.8x and net margin of 54.7% is modestly cheap relative to NMIH (~8-9x) and Radian/MGIC (~7-9x P/E), with a stronger ROE profile. The DCF and EPV MoS (0.67/0.51) confirm intrinsic value is well above market; revenue is flat (+0%) as the mortgage market remains compressed, but the business is highly capital-generative at current credit loss levels. Price is 19% above its 52-week low and below sma200, suggesting the market is pricing in cyclical risk (mortgage volume decline, potential credit normalisation). The cheap-vs-peers verdict is supported by structural quality—Essent has the lowest credit losses in the MI sector—that is not adequately reflected in its relative price.
ESNT.rationale: US private mortgage insurers (NMIH, MTG, RDN) are the direct comparables; Essent screens cheaper than peers on p/FCF and P/E despite superior credit discipline and ROE, making this a quality-at-discount situation.

FHI.relative_comps: Federated Hermes (US/UK active asset manager with ESG franchise) at p/FCF ~14.5x is fairly valued relative to boutique peers like Artisan Partners (~11x) and AMG (~8-9x), and rich versus scale players BEN/IVZ (~8-10x), reflecting the premium ESG/active equity positioning. Revenue grew +11% and the 3-year CAGR is +7.8%, meaningfully ahead of the passive-disrupted peer group; net margin of 22.3% and ROIC of 15.4% are top-quartile. Graham MoS (0.77) and IV15 MoS (0.64) signal the screen finds value; price is near its 52-week high (88% up from trough) and well above sma200. Overall, the multiple is in-line to slightly rich versus pure-play boutiques but cheap versus the quality of the ESG/stewardship moat—mispricing is modest and justified by outperformance.
FHI.rationale: Mid-cap active asset managers (Artisan, Virtus, AMG, Silvercrest, SEI) are true peers; FHI sits in the middle of the peer valuation range, with the ESG moat offsetting active-management industry headwinds.

HNR1.DE.relative_comps: Hannover Re (world's third-largest reinsurer) shows anomalous revenue data (+909%) almost certainly reflecting a re-classification or netting change rather than organic growth; the DCF MoS of 0.72 and IV15 MoS of 0.88 are among the strongest in the universe. p/FCF is reported as 0 (negative free cash in the measurement period), but owner-earnings and Graham MoS are not populated, limiting direct comparison. Against Munich Re (p/FCF ~49x, convergence-screened) and Swiss Re (p/FCF ~11x), Hannover Re screens substantially cheaper on every method where data is available. Net margin of 9.9% is consistent with the reinsurance peer group. Price is near its 52-week low (6% above trough) and below sma200, creating a momentum headwind; however the MoS gap versus peers of similar scale and quality (Munich Re, Swiss Re) is not fundamentally justified.
HNR1.DE.rationale: Global reinsurance oligopoly (Munich Re, Swiss Re, SCOR, Everest Re, RenaissanceRe) are the true comparables; Hannover Re's MoS spread is materially wider than peers with similar underwriting quality and franchise, indicating genuine undervaluation.

MUV2.DE.relative_comps: Munich Re (world's largest reinsurer) at p/FCF ~48.7x appears optically expensive versus global P&C/reinsurance peers trading at 10-20x, but the FCF metric is distorted by large claims reserves and investment portfolio fluctuations common in reinsurance. EPV MoS (0.36), Graham MoS (0.65), and owner-earnings MoS (0.39) all signal intrinsic value above market, and the convergence methodology triggered. Revenue grew +64% (likely reflects prior-year large loss comparisons or premium rate increases) and eps +10%. Net margin of 8.8% is consistent with peers. Price is 6% above its 52-week low and below sma200—momentum is poor despite solid fundamentals. In-line to slightly cheap versus global reinsurers on normalised combined-ratio-adjusted metrics; the 'rich' p/FCF optics are misleading.
MUV2.DE.rationale: Munich Re's true peers are the global reinsurance leaders (Swiss Re, Hannover Re, SCOR, Everest, RNR); on P/B and normalised ROE basis MUV2 is in-line, with its scale and investment management franchise partially justifying a small premium.

ORI.relative_comps: Old Republic International (US diversified insurer: title, general/commercial P&C) at p/FCF ~8.2x and net margin 10.3% sits at the cheap end of the US diversified insurer peer group—Chubb trades at 14-16x, Travelers at 13-15x, WR Berkley at 18-22x. The DCF MoS (0.62), Graham MoS (0.75), and EPV MoS (0.42) are among the strongest in the group. Revenue grew +10% and eps +15%, comfortably ahead of the sector median. Price is 23% above its 52-week low with price below sma200 (momentum lagging). ORI's discount to the large-cap P&C leaders is not justified by underwriting quality or earnings consistency—it is a genuine mispricing largely attributable to lower investor awareness of the title insurance unit's cyclical recovery and the lack of analyst coverage.
ORI.rationale: US diversified P&C and title insurers (Chubb, Travelers, WR Berkley, Cincinnati Financial, Markel) are the correct peer set; ORI screens cheap on every value dimension against this group despite comparable underwriting profitability.

OTF.relative_comps: Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OTF) is a Business Development Company (BDC) lending to middle-market private companies; p/FCF ~5.0x and net margin of 67% reflect the pass-through structure that distributes nearly all net investment income. Revenue doubling year-on-year reflects the scaled-up portfolio from the 2023-2024 capital raise cycle. Against peers ARCC (p/NII ~9-10x), MAIN (~10-11x), and BXSL (~9x), OTF screens cheap—graham MoS (0.86) and IV15 MoS (0.80) are exceptional. The ROIC of 1.76 is an artefact of the BDC reporting structure (leverage on small equity base). Price is 7% above its 52-week low, lagging the BDC sector rerating. The discount vs. Ares Capital and Blackstone Secured Lending is not fully explained by credit quality; OTF should narrow the gap as track record builds.
OTF.rationale: BDC peers (ARCC, MAIN, BXSL, GBDC, OBDC) are the only true comparables given the pass-through, floating-rate credit structure; OTF screens cheap on p/NII and P/NAV versus the sector average.

PRI.relative_comps: Primerica (US term life insurance + financial products for middle-income households) at p/FCF ~9.9x and net margin of 23.3% is cheap relative to Globe Life (~14x), MetLife (~11x), and Principal (~12x), and cheap in absolute terms given 67% eps growth. The convergence signal and Graham MoS (0.65) / IV15 MoS (0.71) are strong; ROIC of 6.38 reflects regulatory capital leverage, which is normal for life insurers. Revenue grew only +4% but eps surged +67% as investment spreads widened and buybacks reduced share count. Price is 62% above its 52-week low and well above sma200—strong momentum. PRI is unambiguously cheap vs. peers on fundamental metrics; the gap is not justified by any quality discount (PRI has lower catastrophe exposure and simpler product mix than MET/PRU).
PRI.rationale: US life/term insurers with middle-market distribution (Globe Life, Unum, Lincoln National, Aflac) are the true peer set; PRI screens discounted despite superior growth, simpler business risk, and strong balance sheet.

SCR.PA.relative_comps: SCOR SE (French global reinsurer, top-6 globally) at p/FCF ~5.0x and DCF MoS of 0.85 / EPV MoS of 0.79 is the most deeply value-screened reinsurer in the universe, and arguably the cheapest in the global reinsurance peer set. Revenue grew +3.6% (modestly, but the eps turnaround is dramatic—up 21,500% from a near-zero base after 2022-2023 loss-year pain). Net margin of 5.2% is below Swiss Re (9.9%) and Munich Re (8.8%), reflecting still-elevated large losses, but the trend is sharply improving. Price is 64% above its 52-week low and above sma200—the rerating is underway. The discount to MUV2 and SREN is partially justified by higher loss volatility history, but given the reserve adequacy rebuilt since 2023, the current gap is excessive.
SCR.PA.rationale: Global reinsurance oligopoly (Munich Re, Swiss Re, Hannover Re, Everest, RenaissanceRe) are the true peers; SCOR screens the cheapest of the group on DCF/EPV MoS despite having completed its financial recovery, making it the highest-conviction cheapness in this sub-sector.

SEZL.relative_comps: Sezzle (US BNPL/instalment payments for retail) at p/FCF ~19.9x is modestly rich versus more established fintech peers Affirm (~15-25x revenue, not yet well-priced on FCF) and Shift4 (~20x FCF), but cheap relative to the hypergrowth cohort given 66% revenue growth and 30% net margin—exceptionally high for the sector. The ev_gross_profit method triggered (gross margin 85%), underscoring the scalable software-like economics. All fundamental MoS methods return -1 because the screen cannot value high-growth fintech on traditional metrics; this is a momentum/growth name, not a value one. Price is near mid-52-week-range (48% above trough). Against BNPL-adjacent peers the multiple looks in-line; compared to pure-play lenders the growth premium is partially justified but requires continued credit quality discipline.
SEZL.rationale: True peers are BNPL/instalment lenders (Affirm, PayPal BNPL, Zip, Latitude) and merchant-facing fintech (Shift4, Nuvei); SEZL is priced in-line for its growth trajectory but with upside if net margin compounding continues.

SREN.SW.relative_comps: Swiss Re (world's second-largest reinsurer) at p/FCF ~11.2x and Graham MoS (0.37) / IV15 MoS (0.73) / owner-earnings MoS (0.33) is the 'middle' of the global reinsurance quartet in the universe—cheaper than Munich Re on p/FCF but more expensive than SCOR and Hannover Re on MoS metrics. Revenue grew +7.4% and eps +54% as prior reserve strengthening reversed; net margin of 9.9% is above SCOR and in-line with Hannover Re. Price is 10% above its 52-week low and well below sma200, indicating poor momentum despite improving fundamentals—the gap between price and intrinsic value is widening. Swiss Re is cheap-to-in-line versus the global reinsurance peer group; the below-sma200 price flags the market is still cautious on large-cat exposure.
SREN.SW.rationale: Global top-tier reinsurers (Munich Re, Hannover Re, SCOR, Everest, RenaissanceRe) are the right peer set; Swiss Re screens cheap relative to peers of similar franchise quality, especially given the 54% eps recovery and 7% premium growth.

TLX.DE.relative_comps: Talanx (German diversified insurer/reinsurer via HDI; parent of Hannover Re 50.2% stake) at p/FCF ~3.0x and DCF MoS of 0.82 / IV15 MoS of 0.92 is the single most deeply cheap name in the Financial Services group on traditional value metrics. Revenue grew +124% year-on-year (reflecting full consolidation of entities and premium growth across industrial lines, retail, and reinsurance); eps +25%. Net margin of 4.3% appears thin but is consistent with a composite insurer with significant reinsurance drag from claims volatility. Price is near its 52-week low (1.3% above trough) and substantially below sma200—poor momentum. The combination of sub-4x p/FCF, 82% DCF MoS, and 92% IV15 MoS versus Allianz, Munich Re, and Zurich (all trading at 10-18x) constitutes a clear mispricing, not a justified discount.
TLX.DE.rationale: German/European composite insurers and conglomerates (Allianz, Munich Re, Zurich, AXA, Generali, HDI/Talanx subsidiary peers) are the true benchmarks; TLX is outlier-cheap on every value dimension against that cohort.

UBSI.relative_comps: United Bankshares (US regional bank, Mid-Atlantic/Southeast) at p/FCF ~12.9x and net margin of 25.5% is fairly priced relative to comparable-scale regional banks—WAFD, Independent Bank (IBOC), and Glacier all trade at 10-14x earnings. Graham MoS (0.39) and EPV MoS (0.29) are modest; owner-earnings MoS is negative, suggesting the screen views sustainable earnings as below reported. Revenue grew +12% and eps +19%, above peer averages driven by NIM expansion from the rate cycle. Price is near its 52-week high (83% above trough) and well above sma200—strong momentum. UBSI is in-line with its regional bank peer set; the positive momentum and above-peer eps growth argue for maintenance of the current multiple but leave limited upside from valuation expansion alone.
UBSI.rationale: Mid-size US regional banks with similar geography and asset base (WesBanco, Glacier, Heartland, TowneBank, Banner) are the true peers; UBSI is fairly priced in that cohort with earnings tailwinds offsetting the lack of deep value.

ARLP.relative_comps: ARLP is a coal MLP serving power-generation (thermal) and metallurgical end-markets; true peers are ARCH Resources (ARCH), CEIX, BTU, AMR, HCC, and NRP. With p_fcf of 8.5x and owner_earnings_mos of 0.39, ARLP screens cheap vs the Appalachian/CAPP peer average which trades 9-14x FCF; it carries a respectable 17.9% ROIC and 14.2% net margin. Revenue is declining (-10.4% YoY, -3.2% 3Y CAGR) in line with secular thermal coal demand destruction, but the MLP structure drives high cash distribution yield which offsets the structural headwind; the discount to peers is partially justified by thermal-coal secular risk but looks real at current pricing — the price sits at 47% of its 52-week range, below the sma200.
ARLP.rationale: Appalachian coal MLP — directly competes with ARCH, CEIX, BTU, and HCC for the same utility and metallurgical coal contracts; NRP is the closest MLP analog. Non-US thermal coal majors Whitehaven (WHC.AX) and Yancoal (YAL.AX) are additional true comparables by end-market.

CNX.relative_comps: CNX is an Appalachian natural gas E&P (primarily Marcellus/Utica) with a large hedge book and capital-return focus; true peers are AR, EQT, RRC, SWN, CTRA, and GPOR. At p_fcf 10.1x and owner_earnings_mos 0.52 CNX looks moderately cheap — Appalachian gas peers average 8-14x FCF currently, so CNX trades at the lower end. Revenue surged +49% YoY (gas price recovery after 2023 trough) but 3Y CAGR is negative (-18%), reflecting commodity-cycle volatility; 29.6% net margin and 47% gross margin confirm good cost structure. Price is at 37% of the 52-week range and below the sma200, indicating a laggard vs gas-price recovery — this looks like genuine undervaluation relative to Appalachian peers.
