CNX.rationale: Appalachian natural gas producer — true comparables are EQT, AR, RRC, SWN, CTRA (all Appalachian gas-weighted E&Ps). The hedge-book strategy and capital-return program align CNX most closely with AR and EQT in terms of business model and capital structure.

DMLP.relative_comps: DMLP (Dorchester Minerals) is a mineral-rights royalty MLP with zero-capex, extremely high-margin cash flows; true peers are VNOM, BSM, TPL, and PHX — all royalty/mineral-rights structures. With p_fcf 9.9x, DCF MoS 0.60, owner_earnings_mos 0.55, and an extraordinary 45.9% ROIC plus altman_z 162, DMLP screens as deep value. Royalty peers trade 15-30x FCF given their no-capex, resilient-cash-flow profile, so DMLP at sub-10x FCF with 37.5% net margin and 46% gross margin looks materially cheap vs the royalty comp set. Revenue is modestly declining (-5.4% YoY) with commodity prices, but the structural royalty advantage is not priced in — momentum is constructive (82% of 52-week range, above sma200).
DMLP.rationale: Mineral-rights royalty MLP — the only true comparables are other royalty vehicles: VNOM (Viper Energy), BSM (Black Stone Minerals), TPL (Texas Pacific Land), and PHX Minerals. Standard E&P or OFS valuations do not apply; zero capex and passive royalty cash flow are the defining economics.

DNOW.relative_comps: DNOW distributes pipes, valves, fittings, and MRO supplies to the oil & gas industry; true peers are MRC Global (MRC), DXP Enterprises (DXPE), and industrial distributors MSM, GWW, and WESCO for valuation context. At p_fcf 11.7x and DCF_MoS 0.55, DNOW sits at a slight premium to MRC (typically 7-10x FCF) but both trade at discounts to diversified industrial distributors (12-20x). Net margin is -3.2% (thin/negative), gross margin 17%, and ROIC 6.6% — all inferior to the industrial distribution comps; the discount is partially justified by energy-market cyclicality and thin margins, but the strong DCF signal implies the stock is not stretched. Momentum is muted — 36% of 52-week range — suggesting lackluster near-term catalysts.
DNOW.rationale: Oil & gas MRO/pipe distributor — MRC Global is the most direct peer by business model and customer base. DXP Enterprises, Applied Industrial, and MSC Industrial are the next tier. Diversified industrial distributors (GWW, WESCO) provide the quality-premium ceiling for valuation.

FSLR.relative_comps: First Solar is the only large-scale US-manufactured thin-film utility solar module maker; true peers are CSIQ, JKS (Chinese crystalline silicon manufacturers), ENPH/SEDG (inverter/microinverter plays), and ARRY (utility-scale tracker). At p_fcf 25.3x with no MoS across methods, FSLR trades at a significant premium to Chinese module makers (CSI Solar, JinkSolar at 5-12x FCF) which is justified by US-content IRA advantages, superior technology moat, and much higher margins (40.6% gross margin vs ~15-18% for Chinese peers). Revenue +24%, EPS +18%, ROIC 8.9%, and a strong upward trend (77% of 52-week range, above sma200) support the premium. Within a fair-value range relative to its true US-solar manufacturing moat, FSLR looks in-line rather than screaming cheap.
FSLR.rationale: US utility-scale thin-film solar manufacturer — only true manufacturing peers are Chinese crystalline-silicon module makers (CSIQ, JKS, DAQO). US domestic content and IRA Section 45X credits create a genuine structural premium vs those peers. ENPH and SEDG are comps by clean-energy end-market but different business model (inverters).

MAU.PA.relative_comps: Maurel & Prom (MAU.PA) is a French listed independent E&P operating primarily in Africa and Latin America (Gabon, Tanzania, Colombia); true peers are Africa/EM-focused E&Ps: Vaalco Energy, Tullow Oil, Pan African Resources, and BTE (Baytex) for business-model analogy. MAU.PA has p_fcf reported as 0.0 (likely cash-flow timing), but owner_earnings_mos 0.53 and graham_revised_mos 0.83 point to deep undervaluation; 70.9% net margin and 33% gross margin are exceptional, and EPS grew +82% YoY. Revenue fell -26% YoY (production/price timing), but the stock trades at 69% of its 52-week range and above sma200. Vs African E&P peers trading at 3-8x owner earnings, the combination of high margins, strong MoS signals, and momentum points to cheap-vs-peers status.
MAU.PA.rationale: African/EM-focused French independent E&P — the true peer set is EM-listed Africa/Latin America E&Ps (Vaalco, Tullow Oil, BTE, CNQ in EM division). TotalEnergies (TOT.PA) provides the quality-premium ceiling as the dominant French-listed oil major.

NBR.relative_comps: Nabors (NBR) is a US and international onshore contract driller; true peers are HP (Helmerich & Payne), Patterson-UTI (PTEN), and PD (Precision Drilling). Within this universe VAL is the offshore driller comp. NBR has p_fcf 0.0 (irregular FCF), but earnings_yield_gap methodology screened it; owner_earnings_mos is slightly negative (-0.07), gross margin 19.5%, net margin 7.8%, ROIC 9%. Vs HP which generates ~30% gross margins and trades 8-14x FCF, NBR's quality is inferior and its heavy leverage (altman_z 1.08, near distress zone) warrants a discount. The stock is trading at 80% of its 52-week range and above sma200 which is constructive momentum. The in-line/slight-discount to Helmerich & Payne is justified by the leverage profile.
NBR.rationale: Land contract driller — HP, PTEN, and PD are the direct operational comps. NBR has more international exposure (Saudi Arabia, Middle East NDS) and materially more debt than HP, explaining its structural discount. Offshore driller comps (NE, RIG, DO) share the OFS cyclicality but different rig type and capital structure.

SBMO.AS.relative_comps: SBM Offshore (SBMO.AS) is an FPSO (floating production storage and offloading) operator and asset developer, a capital-intensive infrastructure play for offshore oil; true peers are BW Offshore, MODEC, Yinson Holdings, and MISC Berhad. At p_fcf 7.0x, graham_revised_mos 0.77, iv15_deep_value_mos 0.75, SBMO looks cheap vs FPSO/offshore infrastructure peers that trade 10-18x FCF given long-term contract-backed cash flows. Revenue surged +28.4% YoY and EPS +558% (contract ramp), with 34% gross margin and 15.6% net margin. ROIC is modest at 6.8% reflecting capital intensity. Price is at 79% of 52-week range and above sma200 — strong trend. The combination of low FCF multiple, two strong MoS signals, surging earnings, and improving momentum confirms cheap-vs-peers.
SBMO.AS.rationale: FPSO operator/developer — the true peer set is FPSO specialists: BW Offshore (BW.OL), MODEC (listed Japan), Yinson (YINSON.KL), MISC Berhad. Broader offshore-infrastructure peers include Teekay Offshore and Exmar. Traditional OFS comps (SLB, HAL) are capital-lighter and not directly comparable.

SHLS.relative_comps: Shoals Technologies (SHLS) makes electrical balance-of-system (EBOS) components — wiring harnesses, combiner boxes — for utility-scale solar; true peers are Array Technologies (ARRY), Nextracker (NXT), and at a stretch ENPH/SEDG (solar electronics). At p_fcf 0.0 (no positive FCF), and all MoS signals at -1 except fundamental_momentum, SHLS is valued on growth: revenue +19% YoY, EPS +43%, gross margin 35%, net margin 7%, ROIC 7.6%. Vs ARRY (15-20x FCF, ~30% gross margins), SHLS is a lower-margin component supplier that commands less multiple. The stock is at 74% of its 52-week range and above sma200. Relative to ARRY and NXT who carry 20-30x FCF multiples for similar utility-solar infrastructure supply, SHLS's lack of positive FCF and thinner margins justify an in-line to slight discount position.
SHLS.rationale: Utility-scale solar EBOS supplier — direct comps are ARRY (trackers), NXT (Nextracker, trackers), and FSLR (modules). These are the same end-market but SHLS competes at the wiring/balance-of-plant layer. Traditional construction/electrical peers (FIX, MYR) provide a quality-premium floor.

SM.relative_comps: SM Energy is a mid-cap Permian/Midland Basin and South Texas E&P; true peers are Civitas Resources (CIVI), Chord Energy (CHRD), Ranger Oil, Magnolia Oil & Gas (MGY), and Northern Oil & Gas (NOG). At p_fcf 6.5x and earnings_yield_gap methodology, SM looks moderately cheap vs Permian E&P peers typically trading 7-15x FCF. Revenue is +18% YoY and net margin 20.5% is solid, but ROIC is severely negative (-97.8% average, distorted by legacy write-downs) and altman_z 0.74 is in the distress zone — these are meaningful red flags that explain the discount. Momentum is strong (80% of 52-week range, sma200 > price signal weak). The low p_fcf discount vs peers is partially justified by leverage and balance-sheet quality concerns.
SM.rationale: Permian/South Texas E&P — the true peer set is US onshore oil-weighted mid-cap E&Ps: CIVI, CHRD, MGY, NOG, VTLE. SM's Midland Basin and Eagle Ford assets compete directly for the same investor dollars as these companies. Devon (DVN) and Pioneer legacy comps provide the large-cap ceiling.

VAL.relative_comps: Valaris (VAL) is an ultra-deepwater and harsh-environment offshore contract driller operating jackups and semis/drillships; true peers are Transocean (RIG), Noble Corporation (NE), Diamond Offshore (DO), and Borr Drilling (BORR.OL). At p_fcf 31x and graham_revised_mos 0.87, VAL trades at a high FCF multiple but the graham signal says it is deeply undervalued on a book/earnings basis — the gap reflects that offshore drillers are earnings-cyclical (improving dayrates driving EPS up 171%) but FCF lags capex. Net margin is an extraordinary 41.5% (favorable dayrate environment), but ROIC is negative (-3.6%) due to high asset base. Stock is at 66% of 52-week range, above sma200 (constructive). Relative to RIG and NE which trade 8-20x forward FCF in a drilling upcycle, VAL at 31x trailing FCF looks rich on that metric but the earnings recovery trajectory and strong MoS signals argue it is in-line on a forward basis.
VAL.rationale: Offshore contract driller (ultra-deepwater/harsh-environment) — direct peers are RIG (Transocean), NE (Noble), DO (Diamond Offshore), BORR.OL (jackup specialist), SDRL (Seadrill). VTOL (Bristow, helicopter transport) is a tangential energy services comp. The offshore driller group is a distinct capital-intensive sub-sector separate from land drillers.

VTOL.relative_comps: Bristow Group (VTOL) provides helicopter transport services to offshore oil & gas operators; the only true direct peer group is offshore helicopter services: Helibus (ERA — now private), HeliService International, Petroleum Helicopters, and Norwegian Air Ambulance for Europe. CHC Group (private) and Cougar Helicopters are private comps. At p_fcf 22.2x with graham_revised_mos 0.80, VTOL screens as undervalued on earnings-power but FCF-expensive. Net margin 8.7%, ROIC 2.4%, revenue +5.3% — modest growth in a niche, capital-intensive business. Vs the offshore-services peer group (VAL, SBMO, WFRD) that trade 7-30x FCF, VTOL's 22x puts it mid-range; the high graham MoS signal vs. the expensive FCF multiple creates ambiguity — classified in-line. Stock sits at 59% of 52-week range.
VTOL.rationale: Offshore helicopter transport — genuinely narrow peer set: CHC Group (private), Era Group, Petroleum Helicopters. By capital intensity and oil-field-services client base it overlaps with WFRD, VAL, NBR but the helicopter-transport model is distinct. Aviation comps (helicopter OEMs like Airbus Helicopters parent Airbus, Leonardo) are manufacturers not operators.

VVV.relative_comps: Valvoline (VVV) is a quick-lube retail service chain with ancillary lubricant brand licensing (lubricants segment sold to Aramco in 2023, now pure-play quick-lube services); true peers are oil-change/quick-lube chains: Jiffy Lube (subsidiary of Shell, private), Take 5 Oil Change (Driven Brands — DRVN), and at a stretch auto-services chains like Monro (MNRO) and Midas. At p_fcf 120.9x with owner_earnings_mos 0.59, VVV carries a very elevated FCF multiple but the owner_earnings valuation sees substantial value — the disconnect is likely due to heavy reinvestment in greenfield store growth suppressing reported FCF. Revenue +5.6% YoY, net margin 12.3%, ROIC 9.2%, and the stock sits at 56% of 52-week range. Vs DRVN (Driven Brands) which trades at 15-25x EBITDA, VVV's premium FCF multiple is partially justified by the asset-light franchising evolution but looks rich on a near-term cash generation basis.
VVV.rationale: Quick-lube retail services — the real peer is Driven Brands/Take 5 (DRVN), not refiners or lubricant blenders. Monro Muffler (MNRO) and Midas/NAPA auto service centers are secondary comps. The Energy sector classification reflects legacy lubricants heritage; the current business is a specialty auto-services retailer growing through franchising.

WFRD.relative_comps: Weatherford International (WFRD) is a diversified oilfield services company (well construction, completion, production, intervention) competing with SLB, HAL, and BKR; the closest analog by size and product breadth is ChampionX (CHX) and NexTier Oilfield (now merged into PFES). At p_fcf 15.7x and convergence-method approval (DCF 0.25, graham 0.66, iv15 0.64), WFRD looks moderately undervalued on a blended basis. Revenue fell -10.8% YoY (OFS cycle softening) and EPS declined -12%, but net margin is solid at 8.8% and ROIC 13.1% is among the stronger in this OFS group. Vs HAL at 12-16x FCF and SLB at 14-18x FCF, WFRD trades at a slight discount that is hard to fully justify given the ROIC recovery story. Price at 76% of 52-week range and above sma200 is constructive. Looks modestly cheap vs the major-OFS peer set.
WFRD.rationale: Diversified oilfield services — SLB, HAL, and BKR are the direct large-cap peers. CHX (ChampionX) and STEP Energy Services are mid-cap comps. WFRD emerged from bankruptcy in 2019 so its post-restructuring balance sheet makes it a pure-play operational comp to HAL's completion/production segment.

AFX.DE.relative_comps: AFX.DE (Carl Zeiss Meditec) is a German precision medtech name in ophthalmic surgical systems and microsurgery, competing directly with Alcon (ALC), Hologic, Haag-Streit, and Topcon, with Thermo Fisher and Danaher as diversified medtech benchmarks. On valuation, p_fcf of 12.7x and a barely-positive DCF MoS (0.04) plus modest owner-earnings MoS (0.32) put it in-line to mildly cheap for a medtech franchise — European medtech peers trade at 15-25x FCF when growing. Revenue growth of +7.8% and gross margins of 52.8% are respectable but net margin of 6.3% and ROIC of 9.2% trail Danaher/Hologic, and the stock is near its 52-week low (proximity 10%), suggesting the market is pricing in continued headwinds from China/macro softness. The discount vs. US peers looks justified by geographic concentration and margin pressure, but not deeply so — closer to in-line on a risk-adjusted basis.
AFX.DE.rationale: True peers are precision ophthalmic/surgical instrument makers (Alcon, Haag-Streit, Topcon) and diversified medtech (Danaher, TMO); valuation is modest for the franchise but margin and ROIC trail the quality tier, making the discount earned rather than a mispricing.

ALNY.relative_comps: Alnylam is the global leader in RNA interference (RNAi) therapeutics, with true peers being Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS, ASO platform), Argenx (ARGX, engineered antibodies), Moderna (MRNA, mRNA platform), BioHaven (BHVN), and larger franchise biotech like Regeneron and Vertex. At p_fcf 87.7x and nearly all valuation MoS scores negative or -1, Alnylam screens rich on traditional metrics, yet the graham_revised MoS of 0.89 (very high) signals the Graham-growth model finds deep value in the accelerating earnings trajectory. Revenue growth of +65% with 81.8% gross margins and a net margin now at 8.4% (recently profitable) represent a rapid transition from loss-making to cash-generating — the stock at 9% proximity to 52-week lows looks anomalous for a franchise of this quality. Rich on trailing FCF multiples, but the growth/margin inflection and a dominant platform moat mean the richness is substantially justified; the proximity-to-52wk-low gap suggests tactical cheapness relative to peers.
ALNY.rationale: RNAi platform is uniquely moated; true peers are other proprietary platform biotechs (IONS, ARGX, VRTX); near 52-week lows despite rapid revenue growth and first-time profitability creates a tactical discount vs. peers at similar or later-stage growth profiles.

ARDT.relative_comps: Ardent Health Services is a for-profit hospital operator — true comps are Tenet Healthcare (THC, also in this universe), HCA Healthcare (HCA), Community Health Systems (CYH), LifePoint Health (private), and Universal Health Services (UHS, also here). At p_fcf of 4.7x and DCF MoS of 0.41, Ardent screens as the cheapest of the hospital trio in this universe (THC at 5.8x, UHS at 11.0x), with the 97.5% gross margin being an accounting artefact of the care-facility revenue-net-of-costs presentation rather than true economic gross margin; real operating margins are thin (net margin 2.1%). ROIC of 4.7% is below cost of capital, trailing HCA (~15%) and THC (~11%), and revenue growth of +6% is on the low end. The low p_fcf is warranted by weak ROIC and thin margins; modest cheapness vs. THC is real but not a quality mis-price.
ARDT.rationale: True comps are for-profit hospital operators (HCA, THC, UHS, CYH); ARDT trades at the lowest p_fcf of its direct peer set but carries below-cost-of-capital ROIC and thin net margins, making it cheap-for-a-reason rather than deeply mispriced.

AXSM.relative_comps: Axsome Therapeutics is a CNS-focused commercial-stage biotech with approved products in MDD and narcolepsy (AXS-05/Auvelity, AXS-12), competing directly with ACADIA Pharma (ACAD), Sage Therapeutics (SAGE), Praxis Precision (PRAX), Harmony Biosciences (HRMY, in this universe), and Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ). Revenue growth of +65.5% with 92.6% gross margins is top-decile for a small-cap CNS commercial launch, but the company remains loss-making (net margin -28.7%, ROIC -89%) as it invests in sales force buildout. No FCF-based valuation is computable; the ev_gross_profit methodology is used. The stock is near its 52-week high (93% proximity), meaning the market already prices in the launch ramp — at this proximity, it screens rich vs. pre-profitability CNS peers at similar revenue stages. The rapid growth is real but most of it appears priced in.
AXSM.rationale: CNS commercial-stage biotech; true peers are ACAD, SAGE, PRAX, HRMY, JAZZ; AXSM's near-52wk-high price combined with ongoing losses and high sales-force burn puts it rich vs. peers at comparable commercial-stage inflection points.

BCRX.relative_comps: BioCryst Pharmaceuticals is a rare-disease/orphan-drug biotech whose commercial product is berotralstat (ORLADEYO) for hereditary angioedema (HAE), competing with Takeda (TEDNF/TKYDY), CSL Behring, Pharming Group (PHARM.AS), and KalVista Pharmaceuticals. Revenue growth of +94% reflects strong HAE market penetration, and gross margins of 97.8% with a net margin of 30.2% show a maturing commercial model. However, the Altman Z of -6 signals severe financial distress (likely driven by accumulated deficit and debt structure typical of rare-disease biotechs). p_fcf of 5.6x is extremely low for a profitable rare-disease franchise — true comps like Pharming and rare-disease platforms trade at 15-30x FCF when sustainably profitable. The distress signal may be accounting-driven (negative book equity) rather than operational; on cash-flow metrics BCRX looks genuinely cheap.
BCRX.rationale: True comps are HAE/rare-disease commercial biotechs (Pharming, KalVista, Takeda HAE franchise); p_fcf of 5.6x with 97%+ gross margins and 94% revenue growth is anomalously cheap vs. rare-disease peers; the Altman Z distress flag is likely an accounting artifact of negative book equity rather than operational insolvency.

CORT.relative_comps: Corcept Therapeutics is a specialty pharma company whose revenue is almost entirely from mifepristone (Korlym) for Cushing's syndrome, placing its true peers among other single-product endocrine/CNS specialty franchises: Harmony Biosciences (HRMY, also in this universe), Invacare, Jazz Pharmaceuticals, and broadly ACADIA Pharma. p_fcf of 61.5x with all DCF/EPV/Graham MoS at -1 signals the market embeds high growth expectations for pipeline (relacorilant) despite a thin approved-product base. Revenue growth of only +12.8% and ROIC of 18.1% are quality indicators, but a 70.5% proximity to 52-week highs means the market is forward-looking. Relative to HRMY (p_fcf 5.4x, faster revenue growth) and INVA (p_fcf 9.7x, 63.8% net margin), CORT trades at a significant premium that is only partially justified by the pipeline optionality.
CORT.rationale: Specialty CNS/endocrine pharma; true comps are HRMY, JAZZ, ACAD; CORT's p_fcf of 61x vs. HRMY's 5.4x and INVA's 9.7x is a steep premium for modestly superior growth — pipeline optionality (relacorilant) explains some of this but leaves residual richness.

HRMY.relative_comps: Harmony Biosciences is a sleep/CNS specialty pharma company (pitolisant/Wakix for narcolepsy/idiopathic hypersomnia), competing with Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ, Xyrem/Xywav), Axsome (AXSM, AXS-12), Avadel (AVDL), and ACADIA Pharma. At p_fcf of 5.4x — the lowest among all specialist biotech/pharma names in this universe — with DCF MoS of 0.61, EPV MoS of 0.08 (barely positive), and owner-earnings MoS of 0.34, Harmony screens as materially cheap. Revenue growth of +21.5%, gross margins of 77.2%, net margin of 18.3%, and ROIC of 18.8% are strong operating fundamentals. Jazz Pharmaceuticals trades at 8-12x FCF; Axsome (loss-making) at far richer multiples. The 44% proximity to 52-week lows adds to the case that HRMY is genuinely undervalued relative to its CNS specialty peer set.
HRMY.rationale: CNS/sleep specialty pharma; true comps are JAZZ, AXSM, AVDL, ACAD; HRMY's p_fcf of 5.4x with 21%+ revenue growth, 18% net margins, and 18.8% ROIC is a clear discount to peers — the cheapness is not explained by quality differentials, suggesting a real mispricing.

INSP.relative_comps: Inspire Medical Systems is a high-growth medical device company in upper-airway stimulation (Inspire therapy for obstructive sleep apnea), competing with Neurocrine Biosciences, ResMed (RMD), Philips (PHIA.AS) in sleep therapy, and structural heart device peers like Shockwave Medical, Nari Medical, and Cardiovascular Systems. Revenue growth of +13.6% (slowing from a 30.8% 3Y CAGR), gross margins of 85.4%, and a first-year of net profitability (15.9% net margin) are strong quality signals. However, p_fcf of 15.6x with only a marginally positive DCF MoS (0.02) and stock sitting near 52-week lows (2% proximity) reflects that the market has already de-rated the growth premium as revenue growth slows. Compared to DXCM or PODD at 20-40x FCF, INSP at 15.6x with improving margins looks cheap on a growth-adjusted basis.
INSP.rationale: High-growth implantable neuromodulation device; true comps are implantable device peers (PODD, DXCM, SWAV, NARI, Medtronic neuromod unit); INSP's p_fcf of 15.6x at 2% proximity to 52-week lows while crossing into consistent profitability represents a discount to growth-device peers, not fully explained by growth deceleration.

INVA.relative_comps: Innoviva is a royalty/specialty pharma holding company earning royalties on Glaxo's respiratory portfolio (Breo, Anoro, Trelegy) plus the Theravance Biopharma stake, making true peers the pharma royalty/licensing tier: Royalty Pharma (RPRX), PDL BioPharma, BioPharma Credit, and specialty holdcos like Jazz and Haleon. At p_fcf of 9.7x, DCF MoS of 0.45, and graham_revised MoS of 0.84 — combined with an extraordinary net margin of 63.8% (pure royalty economics) — Innoviva is one of the highest-quality names in the healthcare group and screens cheap. Revenue growth of +18.5% and ROIC of 14.5% are solid. Royalty Pharma (RPRX) trades at 15-20x FCF; INVA at 9.7x is a genuine discount for a business with near-zero capital intensity. The 70% proximity to 52-week highs is the one cautionary note (momentum already recognized partially).
INVA.rationale: Pharma royalty/holdco model; true comps are RPRX, PDL BioPharma, and specialty licensing franchises; INVA's p_fcf of 9.7x with 63.8% net margins and effectively zero capital intensity is a clear discount to the royalty peer group — mispricing appears real.

MOH.relative_comps: Molina Healthcare is a pure-play Medicaid and marketplace managed care organization, competing directly with Centene (CNC), WellCare (part of CNC), Oscar Health (OSCR), and the large diversified MCOs (UnitedHealth/UNH, Humana/HUM, Elevance/ELV). At p_fcf of 0 (FCF not calculated) and slim valuation MoS scores (dcf 0.02, epv 0.14, owner 0.47), Molina is valued modestly. Gross margin of 9% is typical for MCOs (revenue includes pass-through premiums). Net margin of 1% and ROIC of 15% reflect both the thin-margin business model and capital-light economics. EPS YoY is -56% reflecting elevated medical loss ratios across the Medicaid sector — this is a sector-wide issue. At current prices (~37% proximity to 52wk high), Molina is in-line with peers; Centene and Molina have historically traded at 10-14x earnings vs. the mid-teens for UNH/HUM — the Medicaid-exposure discount is structural.
MOH.rationale: Pure-play Medicaid MCO; true comps are CNC, OSCR, WCG, and the large MCOs (UNH, HUM, ELV); MOH's valuation is in-line with the Medicaid-specialist peer tier and the EPS pressure is sector-wide (elevated MLRs), not a company-specific mispricing.

SAN.PA.relative_comps: Sanofi is one of Europe's three mega-cap pharma companies, with true comps being Novartis (NVS), Roche (RHHBY), AstraZeneca (AZN), Novo Nordisk (NOVO-B.CO), and GSK. At p_fcf of 9.8x, DCF MoS of 0.08, owner-earnings MoS of 0.24, and EPV MoS of -0.11, Sanofi is valued modestly — European big pharma typically trades at 10-16x FCF. Revenue is flat (-1.5% YoY, +2.7% 3Y CAGR), gross margins of 70.1%, net margin of 17.9%, and ROIC of 9.8% are solid but below the AZN/LLY quality tier. The 32% proximity to 52-week highs is neutral. The key risk/opportunity is Dupixent lifecycle and the pipeline (tolebrutinib, amlitelimab) — the market is pricing in some optionality but not as aggressively as AZN or NVO. Sanofi at 9.8x FCF vs. AZN at 20-25x and Novartis at 14-18x is a modest discount consistent with below-peer revenue growth and pipeline depth.
SAN.PA.rationale: European mega-cap pharma; true comps are NVS, AZN, RHHBY, NOVO-B; SAN.PA's 9.8x FCF is a modest discount to peers that is broadly justified by slower revenue growth and a less exciting near-term pipeline vs. AZN/NVO, making it in-line rather than a clear mis-price.

THC.relative_comps: Tenet Healthcare is the second-largest for-profit US hospital operator (after HCA), with true comps being HCA Healthcare (HCA), Universal Health Services (UHS, also in this universe), Community Health Systems (CYH), SurgCenter Development, and Surgery Partners (SGRY). At p_fcf of 5.8x, DCF MoS of 0.44, graham_mos of 0.72, and owner_mos of 0.62, Tenet is materially cheaper than UHS on a p_fcf basis (UHS 11.0x) — yet Tenet's ROIC of 11.2% and net margin of 6.6% are comparable to UHS (ROIC 9%, net 8.6%). Revenue growth of +3.1% and gross margin of 82.3% are typical for the sector. HCA trades at 12-18x FCF with structurally higher margins; THC's 5.8x discount to HCA is partly justified by higher leverage (Altman Z of 1.7 is low) but appears overdone relative to UHS at 11x. THC appears genuinely cheap within the for-profit hospital peer set.
THC.rationale: For-profit hospital operator; true comps are HCA, UHS, CYH, SGRY; THC at 5.8x p_fcf vs. UHS at 11.0x and HCA at 12-18x represents a genuine valuation discount — leverage (low Altman Z) explains part but not all of the gap given comparable ROIC and revenue growth.

UHS.relative_comps: Universal Health Services operates acute-care hospitals and behavioral health facilities (the latter is a more defensible, higher-margin segment), competing with HCA, Tenet (THC, in this universe), Ardent (ARDT, in this universe), and Acadia Healthcare (ACAD) for the behavioral segment. At p_fcf of 11.0x, DCF MoS of -0.08 (slightly negative), graham_mos of 0.85 (very high), and owner_mos of 0.31, UHS shows a mixed picture: rich on DCF but cheap on Graham/earnings yield. Revenue growth of +9.7%, gross margin of 90.4%, net margin of 8.6%, and ROIC of 9% are the highest-quality operating metrics in the hospital trio within this universe. The 4% proximity to 52-week lows — despite solid fundamentals — suggests a meaningful market discount that looks anomalous; UHS near 52-week lows with 9.7% revenue growth and 37% EPS growth is a quality hospital operator at a tactical low.
UHS.rationale: For-profit hospital + behavioral health operator; true comps are HCA, THC, ACAD, CYH; UHS at 11x p_fcf near 52-week lows with 9.7% revenue growth, 90%+ gross margins, and the most diversified/defensive mix in the hospital peer group appears cheap — the behavioral health premium is not reflected in current pricing.
